topics.title: Estimation and Stochastic Process - Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT752 - ADAPTIVE SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation and Stochastic Process - Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Stochastic Process — Formal Definition

A **stochastic process** (or random process) is a collection of random variables indexed by time (or space). It is formally denoted as:

$$X(t, \xi) \quad \text{or} \quad \{X(t) : t \in T\}$$

where:
- $t$ represents time (continuous or discrete)
- $\xi$ represents a sample point from the underlying sample space $\Omega$
- $T$ is the index set (continuous-time $T = \mathbb{R}$ or discrete-time $T = \mathbb{Z}$)

For a **discrete-time stochastic process**, we use the notation $\{x(n)\}$ where $n$ is an integer time index. Each $x(n)$ is a random variable defined on a probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Adaptive filters operate on discrete-time stochastic processes because they are implemented digitally. Hence, the entire module focuses on **Discrete-Time Stochastic Processes (DTSP)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Real-world analogy: The "Stock Market" Metaphor**

Imagine a stock market where the price of a company at any given day is a *random variable* — you don't know exactly what it will be, but it follows a certain probability distribution. Now, the **entire history of prices** over many days forms a **stochastic process**. 

- **One realization** = the actual price curve of one specific company over time (called a **sample function** or **sample path**).
- **Ensemble** = the collection of all possible price curves of *all* companies (or all possible scenarios of one company).

When we say "the value of the process at time $n = 5$", we mean the random variable $x(5)$. The set of all such random variables for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ is the stochastic process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Adaptive Filtering:** We typically only have **ONE observed realization** of the stochastic process. We must use this single observation to estimate statistical properties (mean, autocorrelation, etc.) of the entire process. This is precisely where **estimation theory** comes into play.

### 1.3 Classification of Stochastic Processes

| Classification Criterion | Types | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Index** | Continuous-time (CTSP) vs Discrete-time (DTSP) | $X(t)$ vs $x(n)$ |
| **State Space** | Continuous-state vs Discrete-state | $x(n) \in \mathbb{R}$ vs $x(n) \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| **Stationarity** | Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS) vs Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) vs Non-stationary | — |
| **Ergodicity** | Ergodic vs Non-ergodic | — |

### 1.4 Stationarity — The Cornerstone Concept

A stochastic process is called **stationary** if its statistical properties do not change with time. There are two principal levels of stationarity:

#### 1.4.1 Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS)
A process is SSS if its statistical properties are **invariant under a shift of the time origin** for *all* orders of statistics:

$$P\{x(n_1) \le x_1, \ldots, x(n_k) \le x_k\} = P\{x(n_1+m) \le x_1, \ldots, x(n_k+m) \le x_k\}$$

for all $k$, all $n_1, \ldots, n_k$, all $m$, and all $x_1, \ldots, x_k$.

#### 1.4.2 Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)
A process is WSS if **only the first two moments are time-invariant**:

1. Mean is constant: $E[x(n)] = \mu_x$ (independent of $n$)
2. Autocorrelation depends only on lag $\ell$: $r_x(\ell) = E[x(n)x(n+\ell)]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS** is always true (if moments exist), but the reverse is **NOT** always true. For Gaussian processes, WSS $\Rightarrow$ SSS because Gaussian is fully characterized by first two moments.

### 1.5 Ergodicity — Bridging Ensemble and Time

A WSS process is called **ergodic** if the **time averages equal the corresponding ensemble averages** for *almost every* sample function.

For the mean (Time Average of a single realization):
$$\langle x(n) \rangle_T = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x(n)$$

If the process is mean-ergodic:
$$\langle x(n) \rangle_T = E[x(n)] = \mu_x$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ergodicity matters:** It allows us to estimate ensemble averages (which require many realizations) using time averages (which require only one realization) — a fundamental requirement in adaptive filtering.

### 1.6 Estimation Theory — Formal Definition

**Estimation** is the process of inferring the value of an unknown parameter (or a set of parameters) of a system from noisy, observed data.

An **estimator** $\hat{\theta}$ is a function of the observed data:
$$\hat{\theta} = g(x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1))$$

The estimator is a **random variable** (because it depends on random data), while the **estimate** is its specific numerical value for a given dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction to remember:** "Estimator" = the rule/formula. "Estimate" = the numerical value you actually compute.

### 1.7 Intuition — The Estimation Analogy

**Real-world analogy: "Estimating the average height of KTU students"**

You want to know the true average height $\mu$ of all KTU students (an unknown constant — a *deterministic parameter*). You cannot measure every student. So you:
1. Take a **random sample** of $N$ students.
2. Compute the **sample mean** $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$.
3. $\bar{x}$ is your **estimator**; the value you obtain is your **estimate**.

The question is: *How good is this estimate?* That's where **bias**, **variance**, and **mean square error** come in.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Statistical Parameters of a Discrete-Time Stochastic Process

#### 2.1.1 Mean (Expected Value)
$$\mu_x(n) = E[x(n)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_{x(n)}(x) \, dx$$

For WSS process: $\mu_x(n) = \mu_x$ (constant).

#### 2.1.2 Autocorrelation Function (ACF)
The autocorrelation of a stochastic process captures how a sample of the process at time $n$ is correlated with a sample at time $n + \ell$:

$$r_x(n_1, n_2) = E[x(n_1) x(n_2)]$$

For WSS: depends only on lag $\ell = n_2 - n_1$:
$$r_x(\ell) = E[x(n) x(n + \ell)]$$

#### 2.1.3 Autocovariance Function
$$c_x(n_1, n_2) = E[(x(n_1) - \mu_x)(x(n_2) - \mu_x)] = r_x(n_1, n_2) - \mu_x^2$$

For WSS: $c_x(\ell) = r_x(\ell) - \mu_x^2$.

#### 2.1.4 Variance
$$\sigma_x^2 = E[(x(n) - \mu_x)^2] = c_x(0) = r_x(0) - \mu_x^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study ACF in adaptive filtering?** The autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ (built from $r_x(\ell)$) is the central object in the **Wiener filter** and **LMS algorithm** derivations. The entire adaptive filtering theory revolves around estimating and using this matrix.

### 2.2 Properties of the Autocorrelation Function (WSS)

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Maximum at zero lag** | $r_x(0) \ge \vert r_x(\ell) \vert$ for all $\ell$ |
| 2 | **Even symmetry** | $r_x(\ell) = r_x(-\ell)$ |
| 3 | **Mean-square value** | $r_x(0) = E[x^2(n)] = \sigma_x^2 + \mu_x^2$ |
| 4 | **Non-negative definiteness** | $\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_i a_j r_x(i-j) \ge 0$ |
| 5 | **Limit behavior** | $\lim_{\ell \to \infty} r_x(\ell) = \mu_x^2$ for $\delta$-correlated-like processes |

### 2.3 Power Spectral Density (PSD) — Wiener-Khinchin Theorem

The **Power Spectral Density** $S_x(\omega)$ and the autocorrelation function $r_x(\ell)$ form a **Fourier Transform pair** (Wiener-Khinchin theorem):

$$S_x(\omega) = \sum_{\ell=-\infty}^{\infty} r_x(\ell) \, e^{-j\omega \ell}$$

$$r_x(\ell) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) \, e^{j\omega \ell} \, d\omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $S_x(\omega)$ is **real, even, and non-negative** for a WSS process: $S_x(\omega) \ge 0$ for all $\omega$.

The **average power** of the process:
$$P_x = r_x(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) \, d\omega$$

### 2.4 The Correlation Matrix (Central to Adaptive Filters)

For a tap-input vector $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M+1)]^T$ of length $M$:

$$\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n) \mathbf{x}^H(n)]$$

This is an $M \times M$ Hermitian matrix. For a **WSS** process, $\mathbf{R}$ has a special **Toeplitz** structure (constant along each diagonal):

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} r_x(0) & r_x(1) & r_x(2) & \cdots & r_x(M-1) \\ r_x(-1) & r_x(0) & r_x(1) & \cdots & r_x(M-2) \\ r_x(-2) & r_x(-1) & r_x(0) & \cdots & r_x(M-3) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_x(-(M-1)) & r_x(-(M-2)) & r_x(-(M-3)) & \cdots & r_x(0) \end{bmatrix}$$

Using the even property of $r_x(\ell)$: $r_x(-\ell) = r_x(\ell)$, so $\mathbf{R}$ is **symmetric Toeplitz** (and Hermitian for complex data).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical properties of $\mathbf{R}$:**
> 1. **Hermitian:** $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}^H$
> 2. **Positive semi-definite:** $\mathbf{R} \succeq 0$, so $\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} \ge 0$ for all $\mathbf{a}$
> 3. **Toeplitz** (for WSS process): $R_{ij} = r_x(i-j)$
> 4. All **eigenvalues are real and non-negative**

### 2.5 Properties of a Good Estimator

| Property | Definition | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|---|
| **Unbiasedness** | Expected value equals true parameter | $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ |
| **Consistency** | Estimate converges to true value as $N \to \infty$ | $\lim_{N \to \infty} P(\vert \hat{\theta}_N - \theta \vert > \epsilon) = 0$ |
| **Efficiency** | Has the lowest variance among all unbiased estimators | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \le \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}')$ for all unbiased $\hat{\theta}'$ |
| **Sufficiency** | Uses all information in the data about $\theta$ | $f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta) = g(\hat{\theta} \vert \theta) h(\mathbf{x})$ |

### 2.6 Bias, Variance, and Mean Square Error (MSE)

For an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a deterministic parameter $\theta$:

**Bias:**
$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$$

**Variance:**
$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2]$$

**Mean Square Error:**
$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + [\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})]^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Bias-Variance Decomposition:** $\text{MSE} = \text{Variance} + \text{Bias}^2$. This is one of the most-tested formulas in KTU exams. **Memorize this.**

### 2.7 The Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)

For any **unbiased** estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of $\theta$, the variance is bounded below by:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \ge \frac{1}{I(\theta)}$$

where $I(\theta)$ is the **Fisher Information**:

$$I(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$$

An unbiased estimator that achieves the CRLB is called an **efficient estimator** (or Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator — MVUE).

### 2.8 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) — Intuitive Concept

Given observations $\mathbf{x} = \{x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)\}$ with joint PDF $f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta)$, the MLE is the value of $\theta$ that **maximizes the likelihood function**:

$$\hat{\theta}_{ML} = \arg\max_{\theta} f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta)$$

Taking log and derivative (assuming regularity):

$$\frac{\partial \ln f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta)}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{\theta = \hat{\theta}_{ML}} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Properties of MLE:** (i) Asymptotically unbiased, (ii) Asymptotically efficient (achieves CRLB as $N \to \infty$), (iii) Asymptotically Gaussian, (iv) Invariant under reparameterization.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_x(\ell) = E[x(n)x(n+\ell)]$ | Autocorrelation of WSS process |
| 2 | $c_x(\ell) = r_x(\ell) - \mu_x^2$ | Autocovariance |
| 3 | $\sigma_x^2 = r_x(0) - \mu_x^2$ | Variance |
| 4 | $S_x(\omega) = \sum_{\ell} r_x(\ell) e^{-j\omega \ell}$ | PSD (Wiener-Khinchin) |
| 5 | $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)]$ | Correlation matrix |
| 6 | $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ | Bias of estimator |
| 7 | $\text{MSE} = \text{Var} + \text{Bias}^2$ | Bias-variance decomposition |
| 8 | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \ge 1/I(\theta)$ | Cramér-Rao bound |
| 9 | $I(\theta) = -E[\partial^2 \ln f / \partial \theta^2]$ | Fisher information |
| 10 | $\partial \ln f / \partial \theta = 0$ | MLE equation |
| 11 | $\hat{r}_x(\ell) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert \ell \vert} x(n)x(n+\vert \ell \vert)$ | Sample autocorrelation estimator |
| 12 | $\hat{\mu}_x = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$ | Sample mean (unbiased) |

### 2.10 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

| Application | Use of Estimation Theory |
|---|---|
| **Noise Cancellation (headphones, ECG)** | Estimate noise statistics from reference signal |
| **Channel Equalization (4G/5G)** | Estimate channel impulse response using training sequences |
| **Speech Enhancement** | Estimate PSD of speech and noise for Wiener filtering |
| **Radar/Sonar** | Estimate delay, Doppler from received echoes |
| **System Identification** | Estimate AR/ARMA model parameters from input-output data |
| **Beamforming (antenna arrays)** | Estimate spatial correlation matrix for weight computation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bias-Variance-MSE Decomposition

We start with the definition of MSE for an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a true (deterministic) parameter $\theta$:

$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2]$$

**Step 1:** Add and subtract the mean $E[\hat{\theta}]$ inside the squared term (the classic "add zero" trick):

$$E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2] = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}] + E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^2]$$

**Step 2:** Let $A = \hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]$ (zero-mean random variable) and $B = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ (deterministic constant equal to bias). Then:

$$E[(A + B)^2] = E[A^2] + 2B \cdot E[A] + B^2$$

**Step 3:** Since $E[A] = E[\hat{\theta}] - E[\hat{\theta}] = 0$:

$$E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2] = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2] + (E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^2$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the terms:

$$\boxed{\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + [\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})]^2}$$

This is the **Bias-Variance Decomposition Theorem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric intuition:** MSE measures the *total* deviation of $\hat{\theta}$ from $\theta$. Part of this is random fluctuation (Variance), and part is systematic shift (Bias²). They are independent additive contributions.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Sample Mean is Unbiased

Consider a WSS process $\{x(n)\}$ with mean $\mu_x$ and variance $\sigma_x^2$. The sample mean is:

$$\hat{\mu}_x = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$$

**Step 1:** Take expectation (using linearity):

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x] = E\left[\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} E[x(n)]$$

**Step 2:** Since the process is WSS, $E[x(n)] = \mu_x$ for all $n$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mu_x = \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot \mu_x = \mu_x$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\mu}_x) = E[\hat{\mu}_x] - \mu_x = 0$$

So $\hat{\mu}_x$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\mu_x$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation: Variance of the Sample Mean

Using the same setup as above, let $R_x(k) = E[x(n)x(n+k)]$ be the autocorrelation. Compute $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}_x)$:

**Step 1:** Variance is $E[\hat{\mu}_x^2] - (E[\hat{\mu}_x])^2$. Since $E[\hat{\mu}_x] = \mu_x$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}_x) = E[\hat{\mu}_x^2] - \mu_x^2$$

**Step 2:** Expand $E[\hat{\mu}_x^2]$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x^2] = \frac{1}{N^2} E\left[\left(\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\right)\left(\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m)\right)\right] = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} E[x(n) x(m)]$$

**Step 3:** Use the definition of autocorrelation $E[x(n)x(m)] = r_x(m-n)$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x^2] = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} r_x(m - n)$$

**Step 4:** Let $k = m - n$. The double sum counts the number of pairs $(n, m)$ with $m - n = k$. For each $k \in \{-(N-1), \ldots, (N-1)\}$, there are $N - \vert k \vert$ such pairs:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x^2] = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} (N - \vert k \vert) \, r_x(k)$$

**Step 5:** For **white noise** (samples are uncorrelated), $r_x(k) = \sigma_x^2 \delta(k)$. Only $k = 0$ survives:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x^2] = \frac{1}{N^2} \cdot N \cdot \sigma_x^2 = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{N}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}_x) = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{N} - \mu_x^2 \cdot (\text{term})}\bigg|_{\text{white}} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{N}}$$

(For the white-noise case, the result is simply $\sigma_x^2/N$.) $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}_x) \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$. Combined with unbiasedness, this proves the sample mean is a **consistent estimator** of $\mu_x$.

### 3.4 Derivation: PSD via Wiener-Khinchin Theorem

For a WSS discrete-time process, the average power over the interval $[-N, N]$ is:

$$P_N = \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n) \vert^2$$

**Step 1:** Take expectation. The average power is:

$$E[P_N] = \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} E[\vert x(n) \vert^2] = \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} r_x(0) = r_x(0)$$

**Step 2:** By Parseval's theorem:

$$r_x(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) d\omega$$

**Step 3:** Define the **periodogram** estimator of PSD:

$$\hat{S}_x^{(P)}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2$$

**Step 4:** The expected value of the periodogram is the **circular autocorrelation**:

$$E[\hat{S}_x^{(P)}(\omega)] = \sum_{\ell=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left(1 - \frac{\vert \ell \vert}{N}\right) r_x(\ell) e^{-j\omega \ell}$$

This shows the periodogram is a **biased** estimator of $S_x(\omega)$, with bias vanishing as $N \to \infty$. It is, however, asymptotically unbiased. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Derivation: Cramér-Rao Lower Bound for Sample Mean

Let $x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)$ be i.i.d. samples from $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known and $\mu$ unknown.

**Step 1:** Likelihood function:

$$f(\mathbf{x} \vert \mu) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x(n)-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Log-likelihood:

$$\ln f(\mathbf{x} \vert \mu) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n)-\mu)^2$$

**Step 3:** First derivative (score function):

$$\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - \mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\left(\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) - N\mu\right)$$

**Step 4:** Second derivative:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 5:** Fisher information:

$$I(\mu) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \mu^2}\right] = \frac{N}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the CRLB for any unbiased estimator $\hat{\mu}$ of $\mu$:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \ge \frac{1}{I(\mu)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}}$$

**Step 7:** Check: For the sample mean $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x(n)$, we computed $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) = \sigma^2/N$ in §3.3. So the sample mean **achieves the CRLB** — it is an **efficient (MVUE) estimator** for the mean of a Gaussian distribution. $\blacksquare$

### 3.6 Derivation: MLE for Mean of Gaussian Distribution

From §3.5 Step 2, the log-likelihood is:

$$\ln f(\mathbf{x} \vert \mu) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n)-\mu)^2$$

**Step 1:** Set the score function to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\mu$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) - N\mu = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{ML} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)}$$

**Step 4:** Second derivative check: $\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \mu^2} = -N/\sigma^2 < 0$, confirming this is a maximum. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **The sample mean is simultaneously the MLE, MVUE, and efficient estimator of $\mu$ for Gaussian observations** — a remarkable coincidence that makes it the "gold standard" estimator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: A Stochastic Process — Ensemble View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S["Sample Space (Omega)"]
        direction TB
        O1["Outcome xi_1"]
        O2["Outcome xi_2"]
        O3["Outcome xi_3"]
        O4["Outcome xi_K"]
    end

    subgraph SP["Stochastic Process X(t, xi)"]
        direction LR
        F1["Sample Function 1<br/>x(t, xi_1)"]
        F2["Sample Function 2<br/>x(t, xi_2)"]
        F3["Sample Function 3<br/>x(t, xi_3)"]
        F4["Sample Function K<br/>x(t, xi_K)"]
    end

    O1 --> F1
    O2 --> F2
    O3 --> F3
    O4 --> F4

    subgraph ST["Time Axis t"]
        T0["t = 0"]
        T1["t = 1"]
        T2["t = 2"]
        T3["t = 3"]
    end

    F1 -.Random Variable.-> T1
    F2 -.Random Variable.-> T1
    F3 -.Random Variable.-> T1
    F4 -.Random Variable.-> T1

    style S fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5
    style SP fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00
    style ST fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA
    style F1 fill:#FFE0B2
    style F2 fill:#FFE0B2
    style F3 fill:#FFE0B2
    style F4 fill:#FFE0B2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each outcome $\xi_k$ from the sample space $\Omega$ produces one **realization** (sample function) of the process. The **ensemble** is the collection of all sample functions. At any fixed time $t$, the values $\{x(t,\xi_1), x(t,\xi_2), \ldots\}$ across all realizations form the **random variable** $X(t)$.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Stationarity Hierarchy (SSS → WSS → Ergodic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SSS["Strict-Sense Stationary SSS<br/>All orders of statistics<br/>are time-invariant"]
    WSS["Wide-Sense Stationary WSS<br/>Only mean constant and<br/>autocorrelation depends on lag only"]
    ERG["Ergodic Process<br/>Time averages equal<br/>ensemble averages"]
    GAUS["Gaussian Process<br/>Fully characterized by mean<br/>and autocorrelation"]
    NS["Non-Stationary Process<br/>Statistics change with time"]

    SSS ==>|"implies"| WSS
    WSS -.->|"plus additional conditions"| ERG
    WSS -.->|"if Gaussian"| GAUS
    WSS -.->|"may also be"| NS

    style SSS fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style WSS fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    style ERG fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style GAUS fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px
    style NS fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy summary:** SSS is the strongest. WSS is weaker (but sufficient for most engineering). Ergodic adds the time-average = ensemble-average property. **All practical adaptive filter theory assumes WSS + Ergodic**.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Adaptive Filter — General Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Input Statistics"]
        X["x(n)<br/>Input Signal<br/>WSS Stochastic Process"]
    end

    subgraph SYS["Unknown System / Plant"]
        U["Unknown System<br/>to be Estimated"]
    end

    subgraph AD["Adaptive Filter"]
        W["Taps w_0, w_1, ..., w_M-1<br/>Adjusted by Algorithm"]
        Y["y(n) = w^T x(n)<br/>Filter Output"]
    end

    subgraph ERR["Error Mechanism"]
        D["d(n)<br/>Desired Response"]
        E["e(n) = d(n) - y(n)<br/>Error Signal"]
    end

    subgraph ALG["Adaptation Engine"]
        A["LMS / RLS / NLMS<br/>Algorithm"]
    end

    X --> SYS
    X --> AD
    SYS --> D
    W --> Y
    D --> E
    Y --> E
    E --> A
    A -->|"Update w(n+1)"| W

    style IN fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style SYS fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C
    style AD fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style ERR fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17
    style ALG fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C
    style E fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key idea:** The adaptive filter takes the same input $x(n)$ that drives an unknown system and produces a filter output $y(n)$ that closely matches the desired response $d(n)$. The error $e(n)$ is fed back to adapt the filter weights. The correlation matrix $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H]$ governs the convergence behavior.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Estimation Pipeline — From Sample to Estimate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["True (Unknown) Parameter<br/>theta"]
    M["Stochastic Process<br/>Generates Data"]
    D["Observed Data<br/>x(0), x(1), ..., x(N-1)"]
    ES["Estimator Function<br/>hat-theta = g(x(0), x(1), ..., x(N-1))"]
    EV["Numerical Estimate<br/>hat-theta_N"]
    PR["Performance Metrics<br/>Bias, Variance, MSE"]

    P --> M
    M --> D
    D --> ES
    ES --> EV
    EV -.-> PR
    ES -.-> PR
    P -.->|"compare"| PR

    style P fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style D fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style ES fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style EV fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px
    style PR fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Bias-Variance-MSE Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MSE["Mean Square Error<br/>MSE hat-theta"]
    B["Bias Term<br/>Bias hat-theta squared<br/>systematic error"]
    V["Variance Term<br/>Var hat-theta<br/>random fluctuation"]

    MSE -->|"equals"| B
    MSE -->|"plus"| V

    EX["Example: Sample Mean of<br/>N samples from N mu sigma squared"]
    EX -->|"Bias = 0"| B
    EX -->|"Var = sigma squared over N"| V

    style MSE fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style V fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E
    style EX fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The bias-variance tradeoff:** A great estimator minimizes **both** bias and variance. The CRLB gives a fundamental lower limit on variance. As $N \to \infty$, both bias and variance typically → 0, giving a consistent estimator.

### 4.6 Block Diagram: Wiener-Khinchin Theorem Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACF["Time Domain<br/>Autocorrelation r_x of l<br/>WSS Process"]
    FFT["Fourier Transform<br/>Discrete Time"]
    PSD["Frequency Domain<br/>Power Spectral Density<br/>S_x of omega"]

    ACF -->|"DTFT"| PSD
    PSD -->|"Inverse DTFT"| ACF

    ACF -.->|"Property 1: r_x(0) maximum"| P1
    ACF -.->|"Property 2: r_x is even"| P2
    PSD -.->|"Property 3: S_x real, even, non-negative"| P3
    PSD -.->|"Average power: integral of S_x"| P4

    style ACF fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style PSD fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style FFT fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P4 fill:#E8F5E9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Bridge concept:** The autocorrelation function lives in the **time domain** (lag $\ell$), while the PSD lives in the **frequency domain** (angular frequency $\omega$). The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) is the bridge between them. This duality is foundational in spectral estimation and Wiener filter design.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) process. State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a discrete-time stochastic process to be classified as WSS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A discrete-time stochastic process $\{x(n)\}$ is called **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** if it satisfies two conditions:

**Condition 1 — Constant Mean:** The mean of the process is independent of the time index $n$:
$$E[x(n)] = \mu_x \quad \text{(constant for all } n\text{)}$$

**Condition 2 — Time-Invariant Autocorrelation:** The autocorrelation depends only on the time difference (lag) $\ell = n_2 - n_1$, and not on the absolute time $n_1$ or $n_2$ individually:
$$r_x(n_1, n_2) = E[x(n_1) x(n_2)] = r_x(\ell) \quad \text{where } \ell = n_2 - n_1$$

These two conditions are **necessary and sufficient** for WSS classification. The covariance function is then also a function of lag only: $c_x(\ell) = r_x(\ell) - \mu_x^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** WSS assumption simplifies the analysis of adaptive filters because the correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ becomes **time-invariant** and **Toeplitz**, enabling the formulation of the Wiener-Hopf equation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the bias and variance of an estimator. Derive the relationship between the mean square error, bias, and variance of an estimator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Bias** of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ for a true parameter $\theta$:
$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$$

It measures the **systematic deviation** of the estimator from the true value. An estimator is **unbiased** if $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = 0$.

**Variance** of the estimator:
$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2]$$

It measures the **random fluctuation** of the estimator around its mean.

**MSE–Bias–Variance Relationship:**

$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + [\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})]^2$$

**Derivation sketch:** Add and subtract $E[\hat{\theta}]$ inside the squared term:
$$E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2] = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2] + (E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^2$$
The cross-term vanishes because $E[\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]] = 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define a stochastic process. Distinguish between strict-sense stationarity (SSS) and wide-sense stationarity (WSS) with appropriate mathematical conditions. State the relationship between them. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a WSS discrete-time stochastic process $\{x(n)\}$ with mean $\mu_x = 0$ and autocorrelation $r_x(\ell)$, derive an expression for the **power spectral density** $S_x(\omega)$ using the **Wiener-Khinchin theorem**. State and prove any two important properties of $S_x(\omega)$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Distinction (7 marks):**

**Definition:** A **stochastic process** is a family of random variables $\{X(t) : t \in T\}$ indexed by a set $T$ (typically time). For a discrete-time process, $T = \mathbb{Z}$, and we write $\{x(n), n \in \mathbb{Z}\}$. Each $x(n)$ is a random variable defined on a probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$.

**Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS):** A process is SSS if for all $k$, all $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$, and all $m$:

$$P\{x(n_1) \le x_1, x(n_2) \le x_2, \ldots, x(n_k) \le x_k\} = P\{x(n_1+m) \le x_1, x(n_2+m) \le x_2, \ldots, x(n_k+m) \le x_k\}$$

In simple terms, the **joint statistics of all orders are invariant under a shift of the time origin**.

**Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS):** A process is WSS if only the first two moments satisfy time-invariance:
- $E[x(n)] = \mu_x$ (constant, independent of $n$)
- $r_x(\ell) = E[x(n) x(n+\ell)]$ depends only on the lag $\ell$

**Relationship:** **SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS** is always true (if mean and autocorrelation exist). The converse, **WSS $\Rightarrow$ SSS**, is **NOT** generally true. However, **for Gaussian processes, WSS $\Rightarrow$ SSS** because Gaussian is fully characterized by the first two moments.

**Practical Note:** WSS is the standard assumption in adaptive filter design because:
1. Only first and second-order moments are needed.
2. The correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ becomes time-invariant Toeplitz.
3. It leads to computationally tractable Wiener-Hopf equations.

**(b) Wiener-Khinchin Theorem & Properties (7 marks):**

**Wiener-Khinchin Theorem:** For a WSS discrete-time process, the power spectral density $S_x(\omega)$ and the autocorrelation $r_x(\ell)$ form a Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) pair:

$$S_x(\omega) = \sum_{\ell=-\infty}^{\infty} r_x(\ell) \, e^{-j\omega \ell}$$

$$r_x(\ell) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) \, e^{j\omega \ell} \, d\omega$$

**Derivation outline:** Starting from the DTFT definition and recognizing that the autocorrelation is non-negative definite, we obtain the result that $S_x(\omega)$ must be the DTFT of $r_x(\ell)$. (The rigorous proof requires showing that the inverse DTFT reproduces $r_x(\ell)$ and that $S_x(\omega) \ge 0$.)

**Property 1: $S_x(\omega)$ is real, even, and non-negative.**

*Proof:* Using the even symmetry of $r_x(\ell)$:

$$S_x(-\omega) = \sum_{\ell} r_x(\ell) e^{j\omega \ell} = \sum_{\ell} r_x(\ell) e^{-j\omega \ell} = S_x(\omega)$$

So $S_x$ is even. Using $r_x(\ell) = r_x^*(-\ell)$ for real processes:

$$S_x^*(\omega) = \sum_{\ell} r_x^*(\ell) e^{j\omega \ell} = \sum_{\ell} r_x(-\ell) e^{j\omega \ell} = \sum_{k} r_x(k) e^{-j\omega k} = S_x(\omega)$$

So $S_x(\omega)$ is real. For non-negativity, note that the average power is:
$$P_x = E[|x(n)|^2] = r_x(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) d\omega \ge 0$$
which constrains $S_x(\omega) \ge 0$.

**Property 2: Total average power equals $r_x(0)$.**

$$P_x = r_x(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) \, d\omega$$

This is the integrated spectrum — the area under the PSD curve equals the mean-square value of the process.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define an **estimator**. What are the desirable properties of a good estimator? For a WSS process with true mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$, show that the sample mean $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$ is an **unbiased** estimator of $\mu$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** State and prove the **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** for an unbiased estimator. Hence, show that the sample mean of $N$ i.i.d. Gaussian observations achieves the CRLB. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Estimator — Definition and Properties (7 marks):**

**Definition:** An **estimator** is a rule (function) that maps the observed data to a value that approximates an unknown parameter. Formally, given observations $\mathbf{x} = \{x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)\}$:

$$\hat{\theta} = g(x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1))$$

The estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is a **random variable** (function of random data); the **estimate** is the specific numerical value computed for a given dataset.

**Desirable Properties of a Good Estimator:**

1. **Unbiasedness:** $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ for all $\theta$.
2. **Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_N \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $N \to \infty$.
3. **Efficiency (Minimum Variance):** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \le \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}')$ for any other unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}'$.
4. **Sufficiency:** The estimator uses all information in the data.
5. **Robustness:** Insensitivity to outliers and model mismatch.

**Proof that the Sample Mean is Unbiased:**

Let $\{x(n)\}$ be WSS with $E[x(n)] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}[x(n)] = \sigma^2$. Define:

$$\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$$

**Step 1:** Apply the expectation operator (using linearity):

$$E[\hat{\mu}] = E\left[\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} E[x(n)]$$

**Step 2:** Use the WSS property $E[x(n)] = \mu$ for all $n$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mu = \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot \mu = \mu$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:
$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\mu}) = E[\hat{\mu}] - \mu = 0$$

Hence $\hat{\mu}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\mu$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (7 marks):**

**Statement (CRLB):** For any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of parameter $\theta$, the variance is bounded below by the inverse of the Fisher information $I(\theta)$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \ge \frac{1}{I(\theta)}, \quad \text{where} \quad I(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$$

provided the regularity condition holds: $E\left[\frac{\partial \ln f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = 0$.

**Proof outline:** Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality to the score function $V = \frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}$ and the unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta} - \theta$:

$$[E[(V)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)]]^2 \le E[V^2] \cdot E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2]$$

The left side simplifies using $E[V] = 0$ and $E[V(\hat{\theta} - \theta)] = 1$ (after differentiating $\int f \, d\mathbf{x} = 1$ twice). The first factor is $E[V^2] = I(\theta)$. The second factor is $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$. Thus $1 \le I(\theta) \cdot \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$, giving the CRLB.

**Application to Sample Mean of Gaussian:**

Let $x(0), \ldots, x(N-1)$ be i.i.d. samples from $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known. The joint PDF is:

$$f(\mathbf{x} \vert \mu) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x(n) - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Log-likelihood:** $\ln f = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - \mu)^2$

**First derivative:** $\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x(n) - \mu)$

**Second derivative:** $\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2}$

**Fisher information:** $I(\mu) = -E\left[-\frac{N}{\sigma^2}\right] = \frac{N}{\sigma^2}$

**CRLB:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \ge \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$

For the sample mean $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\text{Var}(x(n)) = \frac{1}{N^2} \cdot N\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

This **equals** the CRLB. Therefore, the sample mean is an **efficient (MVUE) estimator** for the mean of a Gaussian distribution. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "estimator" with "estimate"** — estimator is the rule; estimate is the value. Marks deducted if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the WSS assumption** when proving unbiasedness of sample mean — explicitly state $E[x(n)] = \mu$ for all $n$.
> 3. **Missing the cross-term cancellation** in MSE decomposition — always show that $E[\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]] = 0$ vanishes the cross term.
> 4. **Confusing SSS and WSS** — WSS requires only first two moments; SSS requires all orders.
> 5. **Mis-stating CRLB** — apply only to *unbiased* estimators; for biased estimators, the bound is modified.
> 6. **Missing the "regularity condition"** in CRLB — must state $E[\partial \ln f / \partial \theta] = 0$.
> 7. **Forgetting the factor of $1/(2\pi)$** in the inverse DTFT formula for $r_x(\ell)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

#### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Stochastic process** = family of random variables indexed by time.
- [ ] **WSS conditions:** constant mean + autocorrelation depends on lag only.
- [ ] **SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS** always; **WSS $\Rightarrow$ SSS** only for Gaussian.
- [ ] **Ergodic** = time averages = ensemble averages (allows estimation from one realization).
- [ ] **Autocorrelation** $r_x(\ell) = E[x(n) x(n+\ell)]$ — even, max at $\ell=0$, $r_x(0) = E[x^2(n)]$.
- [ ] **Wiener-Khinchin:** $S_x(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{r_x(\ell)\}$. PSD is real, even, non-negative.
- [ ] **Correlation matrix** $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H]$ is **Hermitian, Toeplitz, positive semi-definite** (for WSS).
- [ ] **Bias:** $E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$. **Variance:** $E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2]$.
- [ ] **MSE = Variance + Bias²** — bias-variance decomposition.
- [ ] **Sample mean $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x(n)$ is unbiased**, with $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) = \sigma^2/N$ for white noise.
- [ ] **CRLB:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \ge 1/I(\theta)$ where $I(\theta) = -E[\partial^2 \ln f / \partial \theta^2]$.
- [ ] **MLE:** Maximize $f(\mathbf{x} \vert \theta)$ w.r.t. $\theta$. MLE is consistent, asymptotically efficient.
- [ ] **Sample mean of Gaussian achieves CRLB** — it is the MVUE for $\mu$.
- [ ] **Fisher Information** is additive for i.i.d. samples: $I(\theta) = N \cdot i(\theta)$ where $i(\theta)$ is per-sample information.
- [ ] **Key distinction:** Estimator = rule; Estimate = value. Parametric (assume model) vs Non-parametric estimation.

#### 🎯 KTU High-Yield Traps to Avoid

1. **Stationarity order:** WSS ≠ SSS in general.
2. **Power vs Energy:** Stochastic processes have *power* (not finite energy); $P_x = r_x(0)$.
3. **DC component:** If $\mu_x \neq 0$, then $r_x(\infty) = \mu_x^2 \neq 0$.
4. **Matrix $\mathbf{R}$** has $r_x(0)$ on the diagonal (mean-square value), not variance.
5. **Sample mean of correlated samples** has *higher* variance than $\sigma^2/N$ — correlation hurts estimation.
6. **Periodogram** is biased (asymptotically unbiased) but not consistent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT752 - ADAPTIVE SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete Time Stochastic Process - Characterisation, Mean Ergodic Theorem, Correlation Matrix - Definition, Properties, Correlation Matrix of Sine Wave Plus Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Discrete Time Stochastic Process (DTSP) — Formal Definition

A **Discrete Time Stochastic Process** is a family (or ensemble) of real or complex-valued random variables $\{x(n)\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$ indexed by an integer-valued time index $n$. For every time instant $n$, $x(n)$ is a random variable defined on a common probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$.

$$X(n) : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{C}, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PERAT752 – Adaptive Signal Processing) treats a DTSP as the fundamental input model for adaptive filters, where the goal is to learn an unknown system driven by such processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Adaptive filters operate on **Wide Sense Stationary (WSS)** discrete stochastic processes. Ergodicity and second-order statistics (mean, correlation) are sufficient to fully characterise such processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **stock market index** measured at the close of every trading day.

* Each **day** $n$ is the discrete time index.
* The **closing value** $x(n)$ is a random variable — you cannot predict it with certainty.
* Over many days, you can compute the *average closing price* (ensemble average) — but you only have **one** market to observe, so you take the *average over time* (time average).

This tension — *"How do I get statistics from a single realisation?"* — is precisely what the **Mean Ergodic Theorem** resolves. If the time average of one realisation equals the ensemble average over all realisations, the process is **ergodic in the mean**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** In DSP, the **second-order moment** $E[\vert x(n) \vert^2]$ is called the **average power** of the process — measured in Watts (W).

### 1.3 Characterisation of a DTSP

A DTSP is **completely characterised** by its joint probability density functions:

$$f_{x(n_1), x(n_2), \dots, x(n_k)}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k) \quad \forall k \ge 1, \; \forall n_i \in \mathbb{Z}$$

In practice (KTU level), only **second-order statistics** are used for adaptive filters:

| Statistic | Definition | Role in Adaptive Filtering |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------------|
| Mean $\mu_x(n)$ | $E[x(n)]$ | DC bias removal |
| Autocorrelation $r_x(k,l)$ | $E[x(k)x^*(l)]$ | Correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ |
| Power $P_x$ | $E[\vert x(n) \vert^2]$ | Diagonal of $\mathbf{R}$ |

A process is **Wide Sense Stationary (WSS)** if $\mu_x(n) = \mu_x$ (constant) and $r_x(k,l) = r_x(k-l)$ (depends only on lag).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ensemble Averages vs Time Averages

For a stochastic process $\{x(n)\}$ we have **two fundamentally different types of averages**:

**Ensemble (Statistical) Average:**

$$\langle x(n) \rangle_{\text{ens}} = E[x(n)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_{x(n)}(x) \, dx$$

**Time Average:**

$$\langle x(n) \rangle_{\text{time}} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x(n)$$

* The **ensemble average** averages across different realisations of the process at a *fixed* time.
* The **time average** averages across time along a *single* realisation.

### 2.2 Mean Ergodic Theorem — Statement

> **Theorem (Mean Ergodicity):** A stationary discrete stochastic process $\{x(n)\}$ is said to be **ergodic in the mean** if the time average of a single realisation converges (in mean-square sense) to the ensemble mean of the process.

Formally, the condition is:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x(n) \xrightarrow{\text{m.s.}} \mu_x = E[x(n)]$$

The **sufficient condition** for mean ergodicity is:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{k=-N}^{N} r_x(k) = 0$$

where $r_x(k) = E[x(n+k)x^*(n)] - \mu_x^2$ is the **autocovariance** function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The autocovariance must "die out" sufficiently fast as the lag $k$ increases. This means **samples far apart in time must be statistically independent**. White noise satisfies this; a pure sinusoid does NOT (because $\lim_{k \to \infty} \cos(\omega_0 k) \ne 0$).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Note |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Mean | $\mu_x = E[x(n)]$ | Constant for WSS |
| 2 | Autocorrelation | $r_x(k) = E[x(n+k)x^*(n)]$ | Function of lag only for WSS |
| 3 | Autocovariance | $c_x(k) = r_x(k) - \vert \mu_x \vert^2$ | Zero mean ⇒ $c_x = r_x$ |
| 4 | Average Power | $P_x = r_x(0) = E[\vert x(n) \vert^2]$ | Diagonal element of $\mathbf{R}$ |
| 5 | Mean Ergodic Theorem | $\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{k=-N}^{N} r_x(k) = 0$ | Sufficient condition |
| 6 | Correlation Matrix | $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)]$ | Size $N \times N$ |
| 7 | Toeplitz Property | $R_{ij} = r_x(j-i)$ | Constant along diagonals |
| 8 | Hermitian Property | $\mathbf{R}^H = \mathbf{R}$ | $R_{ij} = R_{ji}^*$ |
| 9 | Positive Semi-Definite | $\mathbf{x}^H \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x} \ge 0$ | All eigenvalues $\ge 0$ |
| 10 | Sine Wave $r_s(k)$ | $(A^2/2)\cos(\omega_0 k)$ | For random phase $\phi$ uniform $[0,2\pi]$ |
| 11 | White Noise $r_v(k)$ | $\sigma_v^2 \delta(k)$ | $\delta(k)$ is Kronecker delta |
| 12 | Combined r(k) | $(A^2/2)\cos(\omega_0 k) + \sigma_v^2 \delta(k)$ | Signal + uncorrelated noise |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

* **Adaptive Noise Cancellation (ANC):** The LMS algorithm uses an estimate of the correlation matrix to iteratively converge to the Wiener-Hopf solution $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.
* **Spectral Estimation:** The autocorrelation $r_x(k)$ is the inverse Fourier transform of the Power Spectral Density $S_x(\omega) = \sum_k r_x(k) e^{-j\omega k}$.
* **Channel Estimation:** The Toeplitz structure of $\mathbf{R}$ is exploited by the Levinson-Durbin algorithm for $O(N^2)$ complexity instead of $O(N^3)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Ergodic Theorem — Sufficient Condition

**Starting point:** Define the time-average estimate of the mean over a window of length $2N+1$:

$$\hat{\mu}_x(N) = \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x(n)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $E[\hat{\mu}_x(N)]$ (unbiasedness):**

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} E[x(n)] = \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \mu_x = \mu_x$$

So the estimator is unbiased.

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)]$ (mean-square error):**

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = E\left[\left(\hat{\mu}_x(N) - \mu_x\right)^2\right]$$

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = \frac{1}{(2N+1)^2} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \sum_{m=-N}^{N} E\left[(x(n)-\mu_x)(x(m)-\mu_x)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the inner term as the autocovariance:**

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = \frac{1}{(2N+1)^2} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \sum_{m=-N}^{N} c_x(n-m)$$

**Step 4 — Change variables $k = n - m$, count pairs:**

By symmetry, $\sum_n \sum_m c_x(n-m) = \sum_{k=-2N}^{2N} c_x(k) \cdot (2N+1 - \vert k \vert)$.

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = \frac{1}{(2N+1)^2} \sum_{k=-2N}^{2N} (2N+1 - \vert k \vert)\, c_x(k)$$

**Step 5 — Take limit $N \to \infty$ and split the sum:**

$$\lim_{N\to\infty} \text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = \lim_{N\to\infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{k=-2N}^{2N} c_x(k)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the sufficient condition:**

If $\lim_{N\to\infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{k=-N}^{N} c_x(k) = 0$, then $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x] \to 0$, proving mean ergodicity.

$$\boxed{\;\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{k=-N}^{N} r_x(k) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Mean Ergodicity}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Correlation Matrix of Sine Wave Plus Noise

**Given signal model:**

$$x(n) = s(n) + v(n) = A \sin(\omega_0 n + \phi) + v(n)$$

where $\phi \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 2\pi]$ (independent of $v(n)$) and $v(n)$ is zero-mean white noise with variance $\sigma_v^2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the autocorrelation of the sine wave $s(n) = A\sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)$:**

$$r_s(k) = E[s(n+k)s(n)] = A^2 E[\sin(\omega_0(n+k)+\phi)\sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity:**

$$\sin(\alpha)\sin(\beta) = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(\alpha-\beta) - \cos(\alpha+\beta)]$$

$$r_s(k) = \frac{A^2}{2} E[\cos(\omega_0 k) - \cos(2\omega_0 n + \omega_0 k + 2\phi)]$$

**Step 3 — Take expectation over $\phi$ uniform on $[0,2\pi]$:**

$$E[\cos(2\omega_0 n + \omega_0 k + 2\phi)] = 0$$

since $\phi$ uniform $\Rightarrow$ average of $\cos(\cdot)$ over a full period is zero.

$$r_s(k) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 k)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the autocorrelation of white noise $v(n)$:**

$$r_v(k) = E[v(n+k)v^*(n)] = \sigma_v^2 \delta(k)$$

**Step 5 — Use the property that signal and noise are uncorrelated:**

$$E[s(n+k)v(n)] = E[s(n+k)]\,E[v(n)] = 0$$

**Step 6 — Combine all terms:**

$$r_x(k) = E[x(n+k)x^*(n)] = r_s(k) + r_v(k) + \underbrace{E[s(n+k)v^*(n)] + E[v(n+k)s^*(n)]}_{=0}$$

$$\boxed{\;r_x(k) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 k) + \sigma_v^2 \delta(k)\;}$$

**Step 7 — Write the $(N \times N)$ correlation matrix explicitly** (for $N=4$ case):

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} r_x(0) & r_x(1) & r_x(2) & r_x(3) \\ r_x(-1) & r_x(0) & r_x(1) & r_x(2) \\ r_x(-2) & r_x(-1) & r_x(0) & r_x(1) \\ r_x(-3) & r_x(-2) & r_x(-1) & r_x(0) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute the values using $r_x(-k) = r_x(k)$ (real, even):**

$$r_x(0) = \frac{A^2}{2} + \sigma_v^2, \quad r_x(1) = \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega_0), \quad r_x(2) = \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(2\omega_0)$$

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 2\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 3\omega_0 \\[4pt] \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 2\omega_0 \\[4pt] \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 2\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 \\[4pt] \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 3\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 2\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observations:** (i) $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric (Hermitian for complex case). (ii) $\mathbf{R}$ is **Toeplitz** — each descending diagonal from left to right is constant. (iii) Diagonal entries are all equal to $\tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2$ = signal power + noise power.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Proof of Properties of Correlation Matrix

**Property 1 — Hermitian:** $\mathbf{R}^H = \mathbf{R}$

**Proof:**

$$R_{ij} = E[x(n-i+1)\,x^*(n-j+1)]$$
$$R_{ji}^* = \left(E[x(n-j+1)\,x^*(n-i+1)]\right)^* = E[x(n-i+1)\,x^*(n-j+1)] = R_{ij}$$

**Property 2 — Toeplitz:** $\mathbf{R}_{ij}$ depends only on $j - i$.

**Proof:** Follows directly from WSS assumption: $r_x(k,l) = r_x(k-l) \Rightarrow R_{ij} = r_x(j-i)$.

**Property 3 — Positive Semi-Definite:** $\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} \ge 0$ for any $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{C}^N$.

**Proof:**

$$\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a}^H E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H] \mathbf{a} = E[\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H \mathbf{a}] = E\left[\vert \mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{x} \vert^2\right] \ge 0$$

The last equality uses $|\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{x}|^2 = (\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{x})(\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{x})^* = \mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{x} \mathbf{x}^H \mathbf{a}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State the Mean Ergodic Theorem and write its sufficient condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A stationary discrete stochastic process $\{x(n)\}$ is **ergodic in the mean** if the time average of a single realisation equals the ensemble mean.

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x(n) = E[x(n)] = \mu_x$$

**Sufficient condition:** $\displaystyle \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{k=-N}^{N} r_x(k) = 0$, where $r_x(k)$ is the autocovariance function. **(3 marks for theorem + condition + formula)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List any three properties of the correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Hermitian:** $\mathbf{R}^H = \mathbf{R}$, equivalently $R_{ij} = R_{ji}^*$.
2. **Toeplitz:** $R_{ij} = r_x(j-i)$, depends only on lag $j-i$.
3. **Positive Semi-Definite:** $\mathbf{a}^H \mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} \ge 0$ for all $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{C}^N$, so all eigenvalues $\lambda_i \ge 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]`

**a)** Define a Wide Sense Stationary (WSS) discrete stochastic process. State and prove the **Mean Ergodic Theorem** with its sufficient condition. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A WSS process $x(n)$ is the sum of a random-phase sinusoid $s(n) = A\sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ (with $\phi$ uniform on $[0,2\pi]$) and a zero-mean white noise $v(n)$ with variance $\sigma_v^2$, with $s(n)$ and $v(n)$ statistically independent. Derive the autocorrelation function $r_x(k)$ and write the $3 \times 3$ correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks] — WSS Definition:**

A stochastic process $\{x(n)\}$ is WSS if:

$$E[x(n)] = \mu_x \quad (\text{constant, independent of } n)$$
$$r_x(k,l) = E[x(n+k)x^*(l)] = r_x(k-l) \quad (\text{depends only on lag})$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Time Average Estimator:**

$$\hat{\mu}_x(N) = \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x(n)$$

**Step 3 [1 mark] — Unbiasedness:**

$$E[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = \mu_x$$

**Step 4 [2 marks] — Variance Computation:**

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] = \frac{1}{(2N+1)^2} \sum_{k=-2N}^{2N} (2N+1 - \vert k \vert)\, c_x(k)$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Convergence Condition:**

If $\lim_{N\to\infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{k=-N}^{N} c_x(k) = 0$, then $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_x(N)] \to 0$, so $\hat{\mu}_x \xrightarrow{m.s.} \mu_x$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks] — Autocorrelation of sinusoid:**

$$r_s(k) = A^2 E[\sin(\omega_0(n+k)+\phi)\sin(\omega_0 n+\phi)] = \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega_0 k)$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Autocorrelation of white noise:**

$$r_v(k) = \sigma_v^2 \delta(k)$$

**Step 3 [1 mark] — Cross-correlation is zero (independence):**

$$E[s(n+k)v^*(n)] = 0$$

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Combined autocorrelation:**

$$r_x(k) = \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega_0 k) + \sigma_v^2 \delta(k)$$

**Step 5 [2 marks] — $3 \times 3$ correlation matrix:**

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 2\omega_0 \\[4pt] \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 \\[4pt] \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos 2\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos\omega_0 & \tfrac{A^2}{2}+\sigma_v^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]`

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **characterisation of a discrete-time stochastic process** using ensemble and time averages. State the difference between them. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Prove that the **correlation matrix $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)]$ is Hermitian, Toeplitz, and positive semi-definite**. For the signal $x(n) = \sin(0.2\pi n + \phi) + v(n)$, where $\phi$ is uniform on $[0,2\pi]$ and $v(n)$ is zero-mean white noise of variance $0.5$, compute $r_x(0)$, $r_x(1)$, and $r_x(2)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks] — Ensemble characterisation:**

Characterised by joint PDF $f_{x(n_1), \dots, x(n_k)}(\cdot)$ for all $k$. Practical use: mean $\mu_x = E[x(n)]$, autocorrelation $r_x(k) = E[x(n+k)x^*(n)]$.

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Time characterisation:**

For a single realisation, time average $\bar{x} = \lim_{N\to\infty} \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^N x(n)$ and sample autocorrelation $\hat{r}_x(k) = \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^N x(n+k)x^*(n)$.

**Step 3 [2 marks] — Differences Table:**

| Aspect | Ensemble | Time |
|--------|----------|------|
| Domain | Across realisations | Across time |
| Needs | Multiple realisations | One realisation |
| Used in | Theory, Wiener filter | Practical estimation |
| Validity | Always for WSS | Only for ergodic processes |

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Block diagram reference:** (Use diagram from Section 4.2 above.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Hermitian proof:**

$R_{ij} = E[x(n-i+1)x^*(n-j+1)]$. Taking conjugate transpose: $R_{ji}^* = E[x^*(n-j+1)x(n-i+1)] = R_{ij}$. So $\mathbf{R}^H = \mathbf{R}$.

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Toeplitz proof:**

For WSS, $R_{ij} = r_x((n-i+1)-(n-j+1)) = r_x(j-i)$, depends only on lag.

**Step 3 [1 mark] — Positive semi-definite proof:**

$\mathbf{a}^H\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = E[|\mathbf{a}^H\mathbf{x}|^2] \ge 0$.

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Numerical substitution:**

$A = 1$, $\omega_0 = 0.2\pi$, $\sigma_v^2 = 0.5$. So $\frac{A^2}{2} = 0.5$.

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Compute $r_x(0)$:**

$$r_x(0) = 0.5\cos(0) + 0.5\delta(0) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0$$

**Step 6 [1 mark] — Compute $r_x(1)$:**

$$r_x(1) = 0.5\cos(0.2\pi) + 0 = 0.5 \times 0.8090 = 0.4045$$

**Step 7 [1 mark] — Compute $r_x(2)$:**

$$r_x(2) = 0.5\cos(0.4\pi) + 0 = 0.5 \times 0.3090 = 0.1545$$

$$\boxed{\;r_x(0) = 1.0, \quad r_x(1) = 0.4045, \quad r_x(2) = 0.1545\;}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the noise term** at $k = 0$: Many students write $r_x(0) = \tfrac{A^2}{2}$ only — **wrong**, it is $\tfrac{A^2}{2} + \sigma_v^2$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Sign error in cosine lag**: $r_x(k) = \cos(\omega_0 k)$, NOT $\cos(k\omega_0)$ with $k$ inside cosine angle brackets. **Watch the argument carefully.**
> 3. **Assuming $E[\cos(2\phi)] = 1$**: Wrong, it's $0$ because $\phi$ is uniformly distributed. **Lose 2 marks if missed.**
> 4. **Independence vs Uncorrelated**: For Gaussian signals they are equivalent, but the problem statement usually says *independent*. Write **uncorrelated, hence $r_{sv}(k) = 0$**.
> 5. **Mean Ergodic Theorem direction**: Students often write "$r_x(k) \to 0$" instead of the *average* $\frac{1}{2N+1}\sum r_x(k) \to 0$. The theorem requires the **average**, not pointwise convergence. **Critical 2-mark loss.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Discrete Stochastic Process):**

- **DTSP**: Sequence of random variables indexed by discrete time $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **WSS**: Mean constant; autocorrelation depends only on lag $k$.
- **Mean Ergodic Theorem**: Time average = Ensemble mean. **Sufficient condition:** $\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{k=-N}^{N} c_x(k) = 0$.
- **White Noise** is **always ergodic** in mean and autocorrelation.
- **Pure sinusoid with random phase** is **ergodic** in mean (because $\mu_s = 0$) and **NOT ergodic** in autocorrelation (autocorrelation has two discrete frequencies).
- **Correlation Matrix $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H]$** is $N \times N$, Hermitian, Toeplitz (for WSS), and positive semi-definite.
- **Sine Wave Autocorrelation**: $r_s(k) = \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega_0 k)$ — *only* the DC component and the cosine at lag $k$ survive.
- **Noise Autocorrelation**: $r_v(k) = \sigma_v^2 \delta(k)$ — power sits entirely at lag $k = 0$.
- **Combined $r_x(k)$ for sine + uncorrelated noise**: $r_x(k) = \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega_0 k) + \sigma_v^2 \delta(k)$.
- **Diagonal of $\mathbf{R}$** = $E[|x(n)|^2]$ = Total average power.
- **Wiener-Hopf Equation**: $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$ — the cornerstone of optimum linear filtering.
- **Levinson-Durbin recursion** exploits Toeplitz structure: $O(N^2)$ instead of $O(N^3)$ for matrix inversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stochastic Processes — Formal Definition

A **stochastic process** $\left\{x(n)\right\}$ is a collection of random variables indexed by discrete time $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, defined on a common probability space. When its statistical properties are invariant under a shift in the time index, the process is called **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)**. A WSS process is characterized fully by its mean $\mu_x = E[x(n)]$ and its autocorrelation function $r_x(k) = E[x(n)x(n+k)]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Adaptive Signal Processing (PERAT752), every input signal — speech, EEG, radar returns, communication symbols — is modeled as a **real or complex WSS stochastic process** driven by a **white Gaussian noise** $w(n)$ of zero mean and variance $\sigma_w^2$.

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Linear Stochastic Models

The three fundamental **linear time-invariant (LTI)** difference-equation models that generate any WSS process are listed below. They are the building blocks of all parametric spectral estimators.

### (a) AutoRegressive — AR(p) Model
The current sample is a linear regression on its own past $p$ samples plus a white-noise innovation:
$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + w(n)$$

### (b) Moving Average — MA(q) Model
The current sample is a finite linear combination of the present and past $q$ white-noise samples:
$$x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k\, w(n-k), \quad b_0 = 1$$

### (c) AutoRegressive Moving Average — ARMA(p, q) Model
The unified form combining both structures:
$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k\, w(n-k), \quad b_0 = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The symbols $a_k$ and $b_k$ are **deterministic, real coefficients**; the only randomness in the system comes from the driving white-noise $w(n)$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Cocktail" Intuition

Imagine forecasting tomorrow's temperature $x(n)$:
- **AR model** ⇢ *Persistence* — Tomorrow's temperature is a weighted sum of the temperatures on the last $p$ days, plus a small random "weather surprise" $w(n)$. Heat waves and cold spells *echo* through the coefficients.
- **MA model** ⇢ *Impulse Response* — Tomorrow's temperature is purely a delayed reaction to a finite sequence of recent random shocks (e.g., yesterday's cloud burst, the day-before's cold front). The process has **finite memory** of the noise.
- **ARMA model** ⇢ *Hybrid* — Tomorrow's temperature depends on **both** past temperatures (memory) **and** recent shocks (impulses). Most physical signals (speech, vibration, finance) are best captured by ARMA.

## 1.4 Wold Decomposition Theorem

**Herman Wold (1938)** proved that **every** zero-mean WSS stochastic process $\left\{x(n)\right\}$ can be uniquely decomposed as:
$$x(n) = x_d(n) + x_s(n)$$

- $x_d(n)$ is the **deterministic (perfectly predictable)** part — a function of past values with zero innovation variance.
- $x_s(n)$ is the **non-deterministic (regular) part** — equivalent to an **infinite-order MA process**:
$$x_s(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} h_k\, w(n-k), \quad h_0 = 1,\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h_k \vert^2 < \infty$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wold's theorem guarantees that MA$(\infty)$ is universal**: any WSS process *can* be represented this way. AR and ARMA models are merely **parsimonious finite-parameter approximations** of this infinite MA structure.

## 1.5 Complex Gaussian Processes

A **complex random variable** $z = x_r + j x_i$ is **circularly-symmetric complex Gaussian** $\mathcal{CN}(0, \sigma^2)$ if:
$$E[z] = 0, \qquad E[zz] = 0, \qquad E[zz^*] = \sigma^2$$
- The real and imaginary parts are **jointly Gaussian**, **identically distributed**, and **uncorrelated** (hence independent).
- The joint PDF is rotationally symmetric in the complex plane.

In adaptive filtering, all baseband communication signals (QAM, PSK, OFDM) and radar returns are modeled as **circularly symmetric complex Gaussian** because the in-phase and quadrature components are statistically identical.

## 1.6 Power Spectral Density (PSD) — Definition

The **Power Spectral Density** $S_x(\omega)$ of a WSS process is the **Fourier transform of its autocorrelation sequence** $r_x(k)$:
$$S_x(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r_x(k)\, e^{-j\omega k}$$

This is the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**, and it is the bridge between the **time domain** (correlations) and the **frequency domain** (power distribution).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transfer-Function View of Linear Models

Each linear stochastic model admits a rational transfer function $H(z)$ that filters a white-noise input $w(n)$ to produce $x(n)$.

### AR(p) — All-Pole Filter
$$H_{AR}(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$
Stability ⇢ **all poles strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z_k \vert < 1$.

### MA(q) — All-Zero Filter
$$H_{MA}(z) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k z^{-k}$$

### ARMA(p, q) — Pole-Zero Filter
$$H_{ARMA}(z) = \frac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

## 2.2 Why the Three Structures? — Operational Logic

| Aspect | AR(p) | MA(q) | ARMA(p,q) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Memory of $x$** | Infinite | Finite (length $q+1$) | Infinite |
| **Memory of $w$** | Finite (one innovation) | Infinite tail of impulses | Mixed |
| **Spectral Shape** | Sharp peaks (resonances) | Broad nulls (anti-resonances) | Both peaks and nulls |
| **Best for** | Speech formants, narrow-band signals | Transient bursts, exponentially-decaying spectra | General-purpose modeling |
| **ACF tail** | Damped sinusoids / exponentials | Cuts off after lag $q$ | Damped sinusoids / exponentials |
| **PACF** | Cuts off after lag $p$ | Tails off | Tails off |

> [!TIP]
> **Yule–Walker equations** provide the linear system that links AR coefficients $a_k$ to the autocorrelation $r_x(k)$:
>$$r_x(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_x(m-k) + \sigma_w^2\, \delta(m), \quad m \geq 0$$
>These are the cornerstone for **Levinson–Durbin recursion** used in lattice filters and linear prediction.

## 2.3 Wold Decomposition — The Underlying Logic

The decomposition is **unique** and is built by the following constructive procedure:

1. **Step 1 — Innovation extraction:** Compute the one-step-ahead linear prediction error
$$e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n \mid n-1)$$
2. **Step 2 — White-noise check:** The innovation $e(n)$ is the **white-noise** component $w(n)$. If $e(n)$ is already white, the process is *purely non-deterministic* and the deterministic part $x_d(n) = 0$.
3. **Step 3 — Causal MA expansion:** If not, project $x(n)$ onto the linear span of $\left\{w(n), w(n-1), \dots\right\}$ to obtain the causal MA$(\infty)$ filter coefficients $h_k$.

> [!NOTE]
> Wold's theorem formally **justifies the use of AR, MA, ARMA models** for spectral estimation: they are all finite-parameter approximations to the universal MA$(\infty)$ representation.

## 2.4 Complex Gaussian Processes — Deeper Properties

Let $z(n) = x_r(n) + j x_i(n) \sim \mathcal{CN}(0, \sigma^2)$ be a **circularly-symmetric complex WSS Gaussian** process.

| Property | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|
| **Mean** | $E[z(n)] = 0$ |
| **Variance** | $E[\vert z(n) \vert^2] = \sigma^2$ |
| **Pseudo-covariance** | $E[z(n)\,z(n)] = 0$ |
| **Proper / Circular** | Real and imaginary parts are i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2/2)$ |
| **Improper / Non-circular** | $E[z(n)z(n)] \neq 0$ — real and imaginary parts are correlated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **adaptive beamforming** and **MIMO channel estimation**, signals are usually **improper** (non-circular), so engineers use the **augmented complex statistics** $E[z(n)z(n)]$ alongside $E[z(n)z^*(n)]$ — a topic covered in advanced array processing.

## 2.5 Power Spectral Density — Complete Formula Sheet

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Equation | Key Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Wiener–Khinchin (ACF → PSD)** | $S_x(\omega) = \sum_k r_x(k) e^{-j\omega k}$ | Fourier series of $r_x(k)$ |
| **Inverse** | $r_x(k) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) e^{j\omega k}\, d\omega$ | Parseval-style inversion |
| **PSD of filtered process** | $S_y(\omega) = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2\, S_x(\omega)$ | Output PSD = $\vert H \vert^2$ × input PSD |
| **AR(p) PSD** | $S_x(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_k a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}$ | All-pole spectral envelope |
| **MA(q) PSD** | $S_x(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 \left\vert 1 + \sum_k b_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2$ | All-zero spectral shape |
| **ARMA(p,q) PSD** | $S_x(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 \dfrac{\left\vert 1 + \sum_k b_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_k a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}$ | Pole-zero spectrum |
| **Mean-Square Value** | $E[\vert x(n) \vert^2] = r_x(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\omega) d\omega$ | Total average power |
| **Periodogram** | $\hat{P}_{per}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X_N(\omega) \right\vert^2$ | Direct FFT-based estimator |
| **Bartlett (averaging)** | $\hat{P}_B(\omega) = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \hat{P}_\ell(\omega)$ | Variance $\sim 1/L$ |
| **Welch (windowed overlap)** | $\hat{P}_W(\omega) = \frac{1}{LU} \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \left\vert X_\ell(\omega) \right\vert^2$ | $U = \frac{1}{L_s} \sum_n w^2(n)$ |
| **Blackman–Tukey** | $\hat{P}_{BT}(\omega) = \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_x(m) w(m) e^{-j\omega m}$ | Windowed ACF estimate |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **complex magnitude**. In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ is used inside the math mode (not in raw markdown) so the markdown table syntax is preserved.

## 2.6 Properties of the Power Spectral Density

1. **Real and non-negative:** $S_x(\omega) \in \mathbb{R}$, $S_x(\omega) \geq 0$ for all $\omega$.
2. **Even symmetry** (real-valued WSS processes): $S_x(\omega) = S_x(-\omega)$.
3. **Periodic** with period $2\pi$ for discrete-time processes.
4. **Autocorrelation and PSD form a Fourier transform pair** (Wiener–Khinchin).
5. **Linearity:** If $y(n) = a\, x(n) + b$, then $S_y(\omega) = \vert a \vert^2 S_x(\omega)$ (mean shifts vanish).
6. **Filtering theorem:** If $y(n) = h(n) * x(n)$, then $S_y(\omega) = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 S_x(\omega)$.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in PERAT752

- **AR modeling** of speech is used in **LPC vocoders** (mobile telephony, GSM/3G/4G codecs) — Karhunen–Loève-type prediction.
- **MA modeling** of vibration and acoustic echoes enables **active noise control** in headphones and cabin damping.
- **Wold decomposition** justifies why **linear prediction** and **Burg's algorithm** are mathematically optimal.
- **Complex Gaussian signals** form the analytical basis of all **wireless channel models** (Rayleigh, Rician fading).
- **PSD estimation** underpins the design of **Wiener filters, Kalman filters, LMS, and RLS adaptive algorithms** — the very heart of this course.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — PSD of an AR(p) Process from First Principles

**Start:** AR(p) model — $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$.

**Step 1:** Take the $z$-transform of both sides, treating the recursion as an LTI difference equation.
$$X(z) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} X(z) + W(z)$$

**Step 2:** Collect $X(z)$ terms on the left.
$$X(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} \right] = W(z)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the transfer function $H(z) = X(z)/W(z)$.
$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

**Step 4:** Since $w(n)$ is white, its PSD is flat:
$$S_w(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 \quad \text{(constant for all } \omega \text{)}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the LTI filtering theorem $S_x(\omega) = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 S_w(\omega)$.
$$S_x(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}$$

> **Interpretation:** Denominator vanishes near the roots $z_k$ of the AR polynomial, producing **spectral peaks** (formants). This is the mathematical reason why **AR spectra look like resonance patterns** of the underlying pole locations.

## 3.2 Derivation — Yule–Walker Equations for AR(p)

**Start:** AR(p) model — $x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) = w(n)$.

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation $E[\cdot]$.
$$E\!\left[x(n-m) x(n)\right] + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, E\!\left[x(n-m) x(n-k)\right] = E\!\left[x(n-m) w(n)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Use the WSS property $E[x(n-m) x(n-k)] = r_x(m-k)$.
$$r_x(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_x(m-k) = E\!\left[x(n-m) w(n)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the right-hand side. Since $w(n)$ is uncorrelated with all past $x(\cdot)$ (strictly, $E[x(n-m)w(n)] = E[w(n)]\cdot E[x(n-m)] = 0$ for $m \geq 1$), and for $m = 0$ the only correlation is with $w(n)$ itself:
$$E[x(n-m)w(n)] = \sigma_w^2\, \delta(m)$$

**Step 4:** Assemble the final Yule–Walker system.
$$\boxed{\,r_x(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_x(m-k) = \sigma_w^2\, \delta(m), \quad m \geq 0\,}$$

> **Insight:** This is a **Toeplitz linear system** of size $(p+1)\times(p+1)$ in the unknowns $\left[a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p, \sigma_w^2\right]$. It is solved efficiently by **Levinson–Durbin recursion** in $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ operations.

## 3.3 Derivation — Wold Decomposition by Linear Prediction

**Step 1:** Define the one-step-ahead linear prediction of $x(n)$ from the past $n-1, n-2, \dots$:
$$\hat{x}(n \mid n-1) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} h_k\, x(n-k)$$
The optimal weights (orthogonality principle) satisfy $E[e(n) x(n-m)] = 0$ for all $m \geq 1$.

**Step 2:** Define the prediction error (innovation):
$$e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n \mid n-1)$$

**Step 3:** If the innovation variance $\sigma_e^2 = 0$, the process is **perfectly predictable** — this is the **deterministic part** $x_d(n)$.

**Step 4:** If $\sigma_e^2 > 0$, the innovation $e(n)$ is provably **white** (uncorrelated across time), and we can write:
$$x(n) = \hat{x}(n \mid n-1) + e(n)$$

**Step 5:** The optimal predictor itself depends only on past innovations by the **innovations algorithm** (Kailath, 1968), giving a **causal minimum-phase MA filter**:
$$x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g_k\, e(n-k), \quad g_0 = 1$$
This is the **non-deterministic part** $x_s(n)$, equivalent to MA$(\infty)$.

**Step 6:** The total Wold decomposition is therefore:
$$x(n) = x_d(n) + \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g_k\, e(n-k), \quad \text{with } \sum \vert g_k \vert^2 < \infty$$

## 3.4 Derivation — Circular Symmetry of $\mathcal{CN}(0, \sigma^2)$

**Step 1:** Let $z = x_r + j x_i$ with $x_r, x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2/2)$ independent.

**Step 2:** Compute $E[z\, z^*]$.
$$E[z\, z^*] = E\!\left[(x_r + j x_i)(x_r - j x_i)\right] = E[x_r^2] + E[x_i^2] = \frac{\sigma^2}{2} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2} = \sigma^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the pseudo-covariance $E[z\,z]$.
$$E[z\,z] = E\!\left[(x_r + j x_i)^2\right] = E[x_r^2] - E[x_i^2] + 2j E[x_r x_i] = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Conclude: $E[zz^*] = \sigma^2$ and $E[zz] = 0$ jointly define the **circularly-symmetric** property.

**Step 5:** The PDF is rotationally symmetric:
$$f(z) = \frac{1}{\pi \sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert z \vert^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$$
For any deterministic phase rotation $e^{j\phi}$, the distribution of $z e^{j\phi}$ is identical to that of $z$ — hence "circularly symmetric."

## 3.5 Derivation — Periodogram and its Bias

**Step 1:** Consider a finite data record $x(0), x(1), \dots, x(N-1)$. The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) is:
$$X_N(\omega) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** The **periodogram** is defined as the squared magnitude of the DTFT, normalized by $N$:
$$\hat{P}_{per}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X_N(\omega) \right\vert^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the expected value of the periodogram.
$$E\!\left[\hat{P}_{per}(\omega)\right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} E\!\left[x(n) x(m)\right] e^{-j\omega(n-m)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the WSS autocorrelation $E[x(n) x(m)] = r_x(n-m)$.
$$E\!\left[\hat{P}_{per}(\omega)\right] = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} r_x(k) \left(1 - \frac{\vert k \vert}{N}\right) e^{-j\omega k}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the triangular (Bartlett) window $w_B(k) = 1 - \vert k \vert / N$ for $\vert k \vert < N$.
$$\boxed{\,E\!\left[\hat{P}_{per}(\omega)\right] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_x(\theta)\, W_B(\omega - \theta)\, d\theta\,}$$

> **Conclusion:** The periodogram is the **true PSD $S_x(\omega)$ convolved with the Fejér (Bartlett) kernel** $W_B(\cdot)$. Hence the periodogram is a **biased but asymptotically unbiased** estimator. However, its **variance does not decrease with $N$** — this is the famous bias-variance dilemma, fixed by Bartlett, Welch, and Blackman–Tukey methods.

## 3.6 Derivation — Variance of the Periodogram (Gaussian Case)

**Step 1:** For a real-valued, zero-mean, **Gaussian** WSS process, the fourth moment factorization gives:
$$E[x(a) x(b) x(c) x(d)] = E[x(a)x(b)]\,E[x(c)x(d)] + E[x(a)x(c)]\,E[x(b)x(d)] + E[x(a)x(d)]\,E[x(b)x(c)]$$

**Step 2:** Apply this to $\hat{P}_{per}^2(\omega_1)$ and $\hat{P}_{per}(\omega_2)$ and evaluate the covariance.

**Step 3:** After lengthy algebra, for a Gaussian process and at distinct frequencies $\omega_1 \neq \omega_2$:
$$\text{Var}\!\left[\hat{P}_{per}(\omega)\right] \approx S_x^2(\omega) \quad \text{(does not vanish as } N \to \infty\text{)}$$

> **Implication:** The periodogram is **inconsistent**. To reduce variance, one must average over **disjoint sub-records** (Bartlett) or **windowed overlapping sub-records** (Welch).

## 3.7 Derivation — Welch's Method

**Step 1:** Divide the $N$-point record into $L$ overlapping segments of length $L_s$, with overlap ratio (typically 50 %).
$$x_\ell(n) = x(\ell D + n)\, w(n), \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, L_s - 1$$
where $D$ is the segment shift and $w(n)$ is a tapering window (Hann, Hamming, Blackman).

**Step 2:** Compute the windowed DTFT of each segment:
$$X_\ell(\omega) = \sum_{n=0}^{L_s - 1} x_\ell(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Average the periodograms of all segments:
$$\hat{P}_W(\omega) = \frac{1}{L \cdot U} \sum_{\ell=0}^{L-1} \left\vert X_\ell(\omega) \right\vert^2$$
where $U = \frac{1}{L_s} \sum_{n=0}^{L_s-1} w^2(n)$ is the window-energy normalization.

**Step 4:** Variance reduction.
$$\text{Var}\!\left[\hat{P}_W(\omega)\right] \approx \frac{1}{L}\, S_x^2(\omega)$$
> Hence **variance shrinks as $1/L$** at the cost of a slight increase in bias (from the window's spectral leakage). This is the **bias–variance trade-off** at the heart of non-parametric spectral estimation.
