# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Linear Stochastic Model Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["White Noise w(n)"] -- input --> node2["H(z) Filter"]
    node2 -- output --> node3["Process x(n)"]
    node4["Coefficients a_k b_k"] -- parameters --> node2
    node5["Variance sigma w^2"] -- drives --> node1
```

> **Reading guide:** White noise $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ enters the linear filter $H(z)$ (all-pole for AR, all-zero for MA, pole-zero for ARMA) and is shaped into the desired process $x(n)$. The coefficients parameterize the spectral shape.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — AR(p) Realization as IIR Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["x(n)"] --> nodeB["Sum Node"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Output x(n)"]
    nodeD["w(n)"] --> nodeB
    nodeE["z to the -1 delay line"] --> nodeF["Multiplier a1"]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeE
```

> **Reading guide:** This is the direct-form-II IIR realization of an AR(p) model. The output is fed back through delay lines and AR coefficients $a_k$ and combined with innovation $w(n)$.

## 4.3 Architectural Flow — PSD Estimation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["Input Samples x 0 to x N minus 1"] --> nodeS2{"Choose Method"}
    nodeS2 -- "Periodogram" --> nodeS3["Compute DTFT and squared magnitude"]
    nodeS2 -- "Bartlett" --> nodeS4["Split into L disjoint segments"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Compute periodogram of each segment"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6["Average over segments"]
    nodeS2 -- "Welch" --> nodeS7["Window and overlap segment"]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8["Compute windowed periodograms"]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9["Average and normalize"]
    nodeS2 -- "Blackman Tukey" --> nodeS10["Estimate ACF r hat of x"]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11["Apply lag window w of m"]
    nodeS11 --> nodeS12["Fourier transform windowed ACF"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS13["Final PSD Estimate"]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS13
    nodeS9 --> nodeS13
    nodeS12 --> nodeS13
```

> **Reading guide:** All four classical PSD estimators share the same final goal: a smooth, low-variance estimate of $S_x(\omega)$. They differ in **bias–variance trade-off** strategy.

## 4.4 Hierarchy — Relationship Between Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeM1["ARMA p q Model"]
    nodeM2["AR p Model"]
    nodeM3["MA q Model"]
    nodeM4["MA infinite Model per Wold"]
    nodeM5["Any WSS Process x of n"]
    nodeM1 -- "q equals 0" --> nodeM2
    nodeM1 -- "p equals 0" --> nodeM3
    nodeM2 -- "high order approximation" --> nodeM4
    nodeM3 -- "high order approximation" --> nodeM4
    nodeM4 -- "exactly" --> nodeM5
```

> **Reading guide:** Wold's theorem says every WSS process is an MA$(\infty)$. AR and MA models are special cases of ARMA, and ARMA itself is a parsimonious truncation of the Wold expansion.

## 4.5 Architecture — Complex Adaptive Filter Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeC1["Complex Input z of n"] --> nodeC2["Tapped Delay Line"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3["Complex Weight Vector w hat of n"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC4["Complex Summer and Error"]
    nodeC5["Desired Signal d of n"] --> nodeC4
    nodeC4 --> nodeC6["Error e of n"]
    nodeC6 --> nodeC7["Complex LMS or RLS Update"]
    nodeC7 --> nodeC3
```

> **Reading guide:** The complex weight vector is updated by the same LMS/RLS rule, but with **Hermitian transpose** $(\cdot)^H$ replacing the ordinary transpose. This is the natural setting for **circular complex Gaussian inputs** in adaptive beamforming and channel equalization.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define an AutoRegressive process of order $p$. Write the Yule–Walker equation for an AR(2) process and explain how the AR parameters are obtained from the autocorrelation function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **AR(p)** process is generated by
$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$$
where $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ is white noise. For $p=2$,
$$x(n) = -a_1 x(n-1) - a_2 x(n-2) + w(n)$$
The Yule–Walker equations are
$$r_x(0) = -a_1 r_x(1) - a_2 r_x(2) + \sigma_w^2$$
$$r_x(1) = -a_1 r_x(0) - a_2 r_x(1)$$
$$r_x(2) = -a_1 r_x(1) - a_2 r_x(0)$$
These are linear in the unknowns $a_1, a_2$ (with $\sigma_w^2$ obtained from the $m=0$ equation), and are solved directly by matrix inversion or **Levinson–Durbin recursion**. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State and explain the Wold decomposition theorem. Why is it important in adaptive signal processing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Wold's theorem (1938):** Every zero-mean wide-sense stationary (WSS) discrete-time stochastic process $\left\{x(n)\right\}$ can be uniquely decomposed as
$$x(n) = x_d(n) + x_s(n)$$
where $x_d(n)$ is the **deterministic** (perfectly predictable) part and $x_s(n)$ is the **non-deterministic** part, representable as a **causal MA($\infty$) process**:
$$x_s(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} h_k\, w(n-k), \quad h_0 = 1$$
**Importance:** It provides the theoretical foundation for using linear prediction, AR/MA/ARMA modeling, and the design of Wiener/Kalman/LMS adaptive filters — all of which exploit the linear innovations representation $w(n)$ guaranteed by the theorem. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the **Yule–Walker equations** for an AR(p) process and show that the AR coefficient vector satisfies the **normal equations** of linear prediction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Start with the AR(p) model:
$$x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) = w(n)$$

2. Multiply both sides by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation. By WSS, $E[x(n-m) x(n-k)] = r_x(m-k)$.

$$r_x(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_x(m-k) = E[x(n-m) w(n)]$$

3. The driving noise $w(n)$ is white and uncorrelated with all past samples, so $E[x(n-m) w(n)] = 0$ for $m \geq 1$, and equals $\sigma_w^2$ for $m = 0$.

4. For $m = 1, 2, \dots, p$ the right-hand side vanishes, giving the homogeneous Yule–Walker system:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_x(m-k) = -r_x(m), \quad m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

5. In matrix form with the **Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix** $\mathbf{R}$:
$$\mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$$
where $\mathbf{R}_{mk} = r_x(m-k)$.

6. These are exactly the **normal equations** of the one-step-ahead linear predictor $\hat{x}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k)$ which minimizes $E[e^2(n)]$, by the **orthogonality principle** $E[e(n) x(n-m)] = 0$. (7 marks)

---

**Q. (b)** For an **AR(2)** process with coefficients $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$ and white-noise variance $\sigma_w^2 = 1$, compute the PSD at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi/2$. Is the process stable? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Check stability: AR polynomial $A(z) = 1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}$. Multiply by $z^2$:
$z^2 - 1.5 z + 0.7 = 0 \Rightarrow z = \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25 - 2.8}}{2} = \frac{1.5 \pm j\, 0.7416}{2}$
Thus $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.75^2 + 0.3708^2} = \sqrt{0.5625 + 0.1375} = \sqrt{0.7000} \approx 0.8367$.
Since $\vert z \vert < 1$, the process is **stable**. (2 marks)

2. PSD formula:
$$S_x(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\vert 1 - 1.5 e^{-j\omega} + 0.7 e^{-j2\omega} \vert^2}$$

3. Evaluate at $\omega = 0$:
$1 - 1.5 + 0.7 = 0.2 \Rightarrow \vert 0.2 \vert^2 = 0.04$
$S_x(0) = 1/0.04 = 25$. (2 marks)

4. Evaluate at $\omega = \pi/2$: $e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$, $e^{-j\pi} = -1$.
$1 - 1.5(-j) + 0.7(-1) = 1 + 1.5j - 0.7 = 0.3 + 1.5j$
$\vert 0.3 + 1.5j \vert^2 = 0.09 + 2.25 = 2.34$
$S_x(\pi/2) = 1/2.34 \approx 0.427$. (3 marks)

> **Conclusion:** Strong low-frequency concentration (PSD $\approx 25$ at DC) and rapid roll-off, consistent with a low-pass AR process. (7 marks total)

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** With the necessary equations, explain the **Welch method** of PSD estimation. How does it differ from the **Bartlett** and **Blackman–Tukey** methods? Mention the bias–variance trade-off in each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Welch's method** divides the $N$-point record into $L$ overlapping segments of length $L_s$, applies a tapering window $w(n)$ (e.g., Hann, Hamming) to each segment, computes the periodogram of each, and averages:
$$\hat{P}_W(\omega) = \frac{1}{L\, U} \sum_{\ell=0}^{L-1} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L_s-1} x_\ell(n) w(n) e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2$$
where $U = (1/L_s) \sum_n w^2(n)$. (2 marks)

2. **Bartlett's method** is the non-overlapping, rectangular-window special case. Variance scales as $1/L$; bias is the convolution with the Fejér kernel. (1 mark)

3. **Blackman–Tukey** first estimates the autocorrelation $\hat{r}_x(m)$ for lags $\vert m \vert \leq M \ll N$, then applies a lag window $w(m)$ and Fourier-transforms:
$$\hat{P}_{BT}(\omega) = \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_x(m) w(m) e^{-j\omega m}$$
Variance $\propto M/N$, bias controlled by choice of lag window. (2 marks)

4. **Bias–variance trade-off** summary:
- Increasing the number of segments $L$ (Welch/Bartlett) **reduces variance** but **increases bias** (each segment is shorter, giving poorer frequency resolution and spectral leakage from the window).
- Increasing the lag-truncation $M$ (Blackman–Tukey) **reduces bias** but **increases variance** (more noisy ACF estimates enter). (2 marks)

---

**Q. (b)** A complex WSS process $z(n) = x_r(n) + j x_i(n)$ is modeled as **circularly-symmetric complex Gaussian** with $E[\vert z(n) \vert^2] = \sigma^2$. Show that $E[z(n)\, z(n)] = 0$ and that the joint PDF is rotationally symmetric. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. By definition, $x_r, x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2/2)$ and are **independent**. (1 mark)

2. Compute the pseudo-covariance:
$$E[z(n) z(n)] = E[(x_r + j x_i)^2] = E[x_r^2] - E[x_i^2] + 2 j E[x_r x_i]$$
Since $E[x_r^2] = E[x_i^2] = \sigma^2/2$ and $E[x_r x_i] = 0$ (independence):
$$E[z(n) z(n)] = \frac{\sigma^2}{2} - \frac{\sigma^2}{2} + 0 = 0 \qquad \text{(3 marks)}$$

3. Compute the variance $E[\vert z(n) \vert^2]$:
$$E[\vert z(n) \vert^2] = E[(x_r + j x_i)(x_r - j x_i)] = E[x_r^2] + E[x_i^2] = \sigma^2 \qquad \text{(1 mark)}$$

4. The joint PDF of $(x_r, x_i)$ is
$$f(x_r, x_i) = \frac{1}{\pi \sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x_r^2 + x_i^2}{\sigma^2}\right) = \frac{1}{\pi \sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert z \vert^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

5. Under a phase rotation $z' = z e^{j\phi}$, $\vert z' \vert = \vert z \vert$, so $f(z') = f(z)$. Hence the distribution is **rotationally symmetric** in the complex plane — i.e., circularly symmetric. (2 marks) (7 marks total)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PERAT752 (Module 1):**
> 1. **Forgetting the WSS assumption** before writing the Yule–Walker equations. Without stationarity, $r_x(m-k)$ is meaningless. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Conflating AR and MA PSD formulas**: students often write $S_{MA}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 / \vert H \vert^2$ (which is the AR form). The MA denominator is **inside** the squared magnitude in the numerator, not in the denominator.
> 3. **Stability check omitted**: AR/ARMA stability requires $\vert z_k \vert < 1$ for **all** poles. A 1-mark penalty applies if stability is not verified when asked.
> 4. **Periodogram = consistent estimator**: this is **FALSE**. Examiners specifically test whether students know the variance does not vanish. Always state the bias–variance dilemma.
> 5. **Confusing "circular symmetry" with "Gaussianity"**: a complex Gaussian can be **non-circular** if real and imaginary parts are correlated. Always state both conditions.
> 6. **Welch vs. Bartlett**: students forget that Welch **applies a window and allows overlap**; both points are essential for full marks.
> 7. **Missing normalization constant $U$** in Welch's formula — loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AR(p)** is an all-pole model: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$; PSD peaks where poles are near the unit circle.
- **MA(q)** is an all-zero model: $x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k w(n-k)$; PSD nulls where zeros are near the unit circle.
- **ARMA(p, q)** is the unified pole-zero model; most parsimonious for general spectra.
- **Yule–Walker equations** are a Toeplitz system relating $a_k$ to $r_x(k)$; solved by Levinson–Durbin in $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$.
- **Wold's theorem** guarantees every WSS process equals a deterministic part plus a causal MA$(\infty)$; this **justifies** linear prediction and AR/MA/ARMA modeling.
- **Complex Gaussian** $\mathcal{CN}(0, \sigma^2)$: characterized by $E[z]=0$, $E[zz^*] = \sigma^2$, and $E[zz] = 0$; real and imaginary parts are i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2/2)$.
- **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**: $S_x(\omega)$ and $r_x(k)$ form a discrete-time Fourier transform pair.
- **PSD properties**: real, non-negative, even (real processes), periodic with period $2\pi$, and equals $\sigma_w^2 \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ for filtered white noise.
- **Periodogram**: $\hat{P}_{per}(\omega) = (1/N) \vert X_N(\omega) \vert^2$ — **biased but consistent? NO** — variance is $\mathcal{O}(1)$, does not vanish with $N$.
- **Bartlett** averages $L$ non-overlapping periodograms: variance $\sim 1/L$, bias from spectral leakage.
- **Welch** adds windowing + overlap: best bias–variance trade-off among classical non-parametric methods.
- **Blackman–Tukey** uses a windowed autocorrelation estimate: explicit control over bias via lag window.
- **Adaptive filtering link**: ALL adaptive algorithms (LMS, NLMS, RLS, APA) implicitly estimate the input PSD or correlation matrix — Wold decomposition underpins their optimality.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme reminder**: Module 1 carries the foundation for Modules 2 (Wiener filter) and 3 (LMS/RLS); a strong grip on this module is essential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Optimum Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT752 - ADAPTIVE SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Optimum Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linear Optimum Filtering** is the statistical design methodology for deriving the optimal linear filter coefficients that minimize the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** between the filter output and a desired (reference) signal. Rooted in the pioneering work of Norbert Wiener (1949) and Andrei Kolmogorov (1939), the theory establishes the mathematical foundation for all subsequent adaptive signal processing algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A linear optimum filter is a linear discrete-time filter whose coefficients are chosen (designed) to minimize the mean-square value of the error signal, defined as the difference between a desired response and the actual filter output, subject to the constraint that the filter is linear (FIR or IIR)."*

Mathematically, the optimum filter coefficient vector $\mathbf{w}_o$ is defined as:

$$\mathbf{w}_o = \underset{\mathbf{w}}{\arg\min}\, J(\mathbf{w}) = \underset{\mathbf{w}}{\arg\min}\, E\{\vert e(n) \vert^2\}$$

where $J(\mathbf{w})$ is the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** cost function, and $e(n)$ is the estimation error.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Smart Volume Control"**
>
> Imagine you are at a railway station trying to hear your friend's voice (the **desired signal** $d(n)$), but it is mixed with the roar of train engines (the **noise** $x(n)$). You cannot eliminate the train noise directly, but you have a **secondary microphone** capturing mostly the noise. You want to design a *smart filter* that processes the noise reference and *subtracts* an estimate of the noise from the noisy mixture.
>
> The **Linear Optimum Filter** (Wiener filter) is like a precisely tuned volume-and-phase controller that adjusts the noise estimate so that when subtracted from the noisy mixture, the leftover "error" (your friend's voice) has the **lowest possible average energy**. The filter learns the *optimum scaling and delay* required to mimic the noise path perfectly, thereby maximally cancelling it.
>
> This is the essence of **noise cancellation**, **echo cancellation**, and **channel equalization** — every adaptive system you will study is essentially an *iterative, real-time attempt* to find the Wiener optimum.

---

## 1.3 The Two Canonical Filter Structures

| Filter Class | Structure | Stability Guarantee | Group Delay |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **FIR Optimum Filter** | All-zero, $M$-tap transversal | Always stable (no poles) | Fixed, $(M-1)/2$ for linear phase |
| **IIR Optimum Filter** | Pole-zero, rational $H(z)$ | Unstable regions exist | Non-linear, frequency-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study both?** The FIR Wiener filter is mathematically tractable (closed-form via linear algebra), while the IIR Wiener filter provides superior frequency selectivity at lower filter orders. Both share the **same MMSE optimization principle**, but differ in the *constraints* imposed on the filter structure.

---

## 1.4 Why "Optimum"? — The Optimization Landscape

The MSE cost function $J(\mathbf{w})$ is a **quadratic function** of the filter coefficients when the input is assumed to be **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** and **Gaussian**. This implies:

- The cost surface is a **convex, bowl-shaped paraboloid** in $(M+1)$-dimensional weight space.
- A **single global minimum** exists (no local minima traps).
- Gradient-based search algorithms (steepest descent, LMS, RLS) will *always* converge to this unique optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The existence of a *single, unique global minimum* is the cornerstone of all adaptive filtering theory. Without this property, gradient descent would be unreliable — convergence would depend on the starting point.

---

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Linear Optimum Filtering Theory

1. **The Mean Square Error (MMSE) Criterion** — defines optimality.
2. **The Orthogonality Principle** — characterizes the geometric optimum (the error is uncorrelated with all input samples).
3. **The Wiener-Hopf Equation** — provides the *closed-form* algebraic solution for $\mathbf{w}_o$.

These three pillars are mathematically equivalent but provide different perspectives: algebraic, geometric, and computational.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Model and Mathematical Setup

Consider a **discrete-time linear combiner** (transversal FIR structure) of order $M$ processing a real-valued, stationary input $x(n)$:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} w_k \, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$$

where:

$$\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_0 & w_1 & w_2 & \cdots & w_M \end{bmatrix}^T$$

$$\mathbf{x}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} x(n) & x(n-1) & x(n-2) & \cdots & x(n-M) \end{bmatrix}^T$$

The **estimation error** (a scalar quantity) is:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$$

The **performance criterion** is the **mean-square value of the estimation error**:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

---

## 2.2 Expansion of the MSE Cost Function (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1:** Expand the squared error:

$$J = E\{d^2(n) - 2 d(n)\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n) + [\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

**Step 2:** Use linearity of expectation and note that $\mathbf{w}$ is deterministic:

$$J = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2\mathbf{w}^T E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\} + \mathbf{w}^T E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the three statistical objects:

- $\sigma_d^2 = E\{d^2(n)\}$ — variance of the desired signal (assuming zero mean).
- $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ — $(M+1)\times 1$ **cross-correlation vector** between $d(n)$ and the tap inputs.
- $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$ — $(M+1)\times(M+1)$ **autocorrelation matrix** of the tap inputs.

**Step 4:** The compact quadratic form emerges:

$$\boxed{J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}}$$

This is a **quadratic function** of $\mathbf{w}$ with positive-definite Hessian $\mathbf{R}$ (assuming the input is persistently exciting).

---

## 2.3 The Orthogonality Principle

Setting the gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \mathbf{0}$ yields the **orthogonality condition**:

$$E\{e_o(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{0}$$

or component-wise:

$$E\{e_o(n)\, x(n-k)\} = 0 \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, \ldots, M$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** At the optimum, the error vector $\mathbf{e}_o$ (in the signal space spanned by the input samples) is **orthogonal** (uncorrelated) to every input sample in the tap-delay line. This is analogous to finding the shortest distance from a point to a hyperplane — the optimal "foot of the perpendicular."

---

## 2.4 The Wiener-Hopf Equation (FIR Closed-Form Solution)

Setting $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \mathbf{0}$ yields the matrix normal equations:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}}$$

This is the **Wiener-Hopf equation** — the cornerstone of linear optimum filtering. It prescribes the unique set of FIR filter coefficients that achieve minimum MSE for a stationary, stochastic environment.

---

## 2.5 Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE)

Substituting $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ back into $J(\mathbf{w})$:

$$J_{\min} = J(\mathbf{w}_o) = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $J_{\min}$ represents the *irreducible* estimation error that cannot be removed by any linear filter. It is the energy of the *innovation* — the part of $d(n)$ that is **uncorrelated** with the entire input history.

---

## 2.6 The Error Performance Surface

The Hessian of $J(\mathbf{w})$ is $2\mathbf{R}$. Because $\mathbf{R}$ is **positive definite** (under persistent excitation), the error performance surface is a **convex hyperparaboloid** ("bowl") with a unique global minimum at $\mathbf{w}_o$.

The eigenvalue spread of $\mathbf{R}$ (ratio $\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$) governs:

- **Curvature** in each principal axis direction.
- **Convergence speed** of gradient descent algorithms (small spread = fast convergence; large spread = slow, ill-conditioned convergence).

---

## 2.7 Extension to Complex-Valued Signals (Widely-Linear / Complex Case)

For **complex baseband signals** (common in communications, radar, and beamforming), the cost function is:

$$J = E\{\vert e(n) \vert^2\} = E\{e(n)\, e^*(n)\}$$

This yields the **complex Wiener-Hopf equation**:

$$\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$$

where $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)\}$ (Hermitian) and $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{x}^*(n)\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transpose $T$ is replaced by the Hermitian $H$ (conjugate transpose). For real signals, $H \equiv T$ and the two formulations coincide.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** that you must memorize for KTU board examinations on this topic. No vertical-pipe characters appear inside table cells to preserve markdown rendering integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Dimension / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Filter Output | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} w_k\, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ | Scalar |
| 2 | Estimation Error | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Scalar |
| 3 | MSE Cost Function | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ | Power (W) |
| 4 | Autocorrelation Matrix | $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$, $R_{ij} = r_x(\vert i-j \vert)$ | $(M+1)\times(M+1)$ |
| 5 | Cross-correlation Vector | $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$, $p_i = r_{dx}(i)$ | $(M+1)\times 1$ |
| 6 | Orthogonality Principle | $E\{e_o(n)\,x(n-k)\} = 0,\ \forall k$ | Scalar equations |
| 7 | Wiener-Hopf Equation | $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$ | Matrix equation |
| 8 | Optimum Weights | $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | $(M+1)\times 1$ |
| 9 | Minimum MSE | $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Power (W) |
| 10 | Performance Surface Curvature | Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{R}$ (positive, real) | W per tap² |
| 11 | Complex Case Extension | $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H\},\ \mathbf{p} = E\{d\,\mathbf{x}^*\}$ | Hermitian matrix |
| 12 | Yule-Walker (for AR process) | $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$ where $\mathbf{a}$ = AR coefficients | $(p\times p)$ matrix |

> **Notation Note:** The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value (modulus). In equations, we have used $\vert i-j \vert$ instead of $\vert i-j \vert$ within table cells for LaTeX safety.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Linear Optimum Filter |
|:---|:---|
| **Speech Enhancement** | Wiener filter for denoising corrupted speech recordings |
| **Channel Equalization** | Remove intersymbol interference in digital communication receivers |
| **System Identification** | Model an unknown plant by minimizing output error MSE |
| **Noise Cancellation (ANC)** | Active headsets, cockpit headsets, MRI acoustic noise reduction |
| **Radar/Sonar Beamforming** | Optimum spatial filtering for target detection in clutter |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | ECG/EEG denoising, removing powerline interference |
| **Predictive Coding** | Linear prediction of speech (LPC vocoders), image compression (DPCM) |
| **Financial Forecasting** | Adaptive prediction of stock return time series |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are presented with **every algebraic transition explicit**. No step is skipped; this matches the expected depth in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions and ensures full marks on derivation-style problems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I: Expansion of the MSE Cost Function into Quadratic Form

**Starting Point:**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - y(n)]^2\}$$

Substituting $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$J = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$J = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2\,E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\} + E\{[\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

**Step 2:** Since $\mathbf{w}$ is deterministic (time-invariant filter coefficients), we can pull $\mathbf{w}^T$ outside the expectation in the middle term:

$$J = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} + E\{\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\mathbf{w}\}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the three scalar/matrix/vector statistical descriptors:

$$E\{d^2(n)\} = \sigma_d^2 \quad (\text{variance of desired signal})$$

$$E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{p} \quad ((M+1)\times 1 \text{ cross-correlation vector})$$

$$E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\} = \mathbf{R} \quad ((M+1)\times(M+1) \text{ autocorrelation matrix})$$

**Step 4:** Assemble the compact matrix expression:

$$\boxed{J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}}$$

This is the **fundamental quadratic form** that defines the geometry of the entire Wiener filter theory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: The Wiener-Hopf Equation via Direct Minimization

**Starting Point:** Quadratic form from above:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the gradient of $J$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$. Recall:
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\sigma_d^2) = \mathbf{0}$ (constant).
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(-2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p}) = -2\mathbf{p}$ (linear term).
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ (using symmetry of $\mathbf{R}$: $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}^T$).

**Step 2:** Combine:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}) = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3:** Set the gradient to zero at the optimum $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}_o$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}_o) = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4:** Solve the matrix linear system for $\mathbf{w}_o$:

$$2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = 2\mathbf{p} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$$

**Step 5:** Pre-multiply both sides by $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ (which exists because $\mathbf{R}$ is positive definite under persistent excitation):

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ guaranteed to exist?** The autocorrelation matrix of a persistently exciting input signal is **symmetric positive definite** (all eigenvalues strictly positive). This is a sufficient condition for invertibility and ensures a unique global minimum exists.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: The Orthogonality Principle (Geometric Derivation)

**Starting Point:** The optimum condition $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \mathbf{0}$ gives $E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 1:** Recall the error definition:

$$e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 2:** At the optimum $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}_o$, substitute $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$:

$$e_o(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3:** Take the cross-correlation $E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\}$:

$$E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1} E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ and $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T\}$:

$$E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}^T = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the **orthogonality principle** is established:

$$\boxed{E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{0}}$$

Equivalently, component-wise:

$$E\{e_o(n)\,x(n-k)\} = 0, \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, \ldots, M$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Constrained Optimization View:** The orthogonality principle is equivalent to projecting $d(n)$ *orthogonally* onto the subspace spanned by $\{x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M)\}$ in the Hilbert space of random variables with inner product $\langle u,v \rangle = E\{uv\}$. The optimum filter output is the **projection**, and the error is the **residual orthogonal to the subspace**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Minimum MSE Expression

**Starting Point:** Substitute $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ into $J(\mathbf{w})$.

$$J_{\min} = J(\mathbf{w}_o) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

**Step 1:** Simplify the third term: $\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$:

$$J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

**Step 2:** Combine the two identical cross-terms:

$$\boxed{J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Uncorrelated Input and Desired Signal:** If $d(n)$ is uncorrelated with $\mathbf{x}(n)$, then $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0}$ and $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2$. The filter cannot reduce the error at all, because there is no linear statistical relationship to exploit.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V: The IIR Wiener Filter (Non-Causal Case)

For the **non-causal IIR Wiener filter**, the optimum transfer function operates on the entire two-sided signal history and is given by the **Wiener-Khinchin spectral relationship**:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{nc}}(z) = \frac{S_{dx}(z)}{S_{xx}(z)}}$$

where:

- $S_{xx}(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{r_x(k)\}$ — power spectral density (PSD) of input.
- $S_{dx}(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{r_{dx}(k)\}$ — cross-power spectral density.

**Step 1:** Derive from the convolution-domain Wiener-Hopf equation. In the $z$-domain, the optimum filter minimizes $J$ subject to the spectral constraint:

$$S_{ee}(z) = S_{dd}(z) - H(z)\,S_{dx}(z^{-1}) - H(z^{-1})\,S_{dx}(z) + H(z)\,H(z^{-1})\,S_{xx}(z)$$

**Step 2:** Take the partial derivative with respect to $H(z)$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S_{ee}}{\partial H(z)} = -S_{dx}(z^{-1}) + H(z^{-1})\,S_{xx}(z) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $H(z^{-1})$:

$$H(z^{-1}) = \frac{S_{dx}(z^{-1})}{S_{xx}(z)}$$

**Step 4:** Replace $z \leftrightarrow z^{-1}$ to obtain the non-causal filter:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{nc}}(z) = \frac{S_{dx}(z)}{S_{xx}(z)}}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation VI: Causal IIR Wiener Filter (Whitening Approach)

For a **causal** filter, $H(z)$ must be analytic (no poles) outside the unit circle. This requires **spectral factorization** of the input PSD:

$$S_{xx}(z) = S_{xx}^{+}(z)\,S_{xx}^{-}(z)$$

where $S_{xx}^{+}(z)$ contains all poles and zeros **inside** the unit circle (minimum-phase), and $S_{xx}^{-}(z) = S_{xx}^{+}(z^{-1})$ contains the mirrored counterparts.

**Step 1:** Define the **causal part** of a spectrum as:

$$[S_{dx}(z)/S_{xx}^{-}(z)]_{+} = \text{causal part of } \frac{S_{dx}(z)}{S_{xx}^{-}(z)}$$

**Step 2:** The **causal Wiener filter** is:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{c}}(z) = \frac{1}{S_{xx}^{+}(z)}\left[\frac{S_{dx}(z)}{S_{xx}^{-}(z)}\right]_{+}}$$

**Step 3:** The corresponding **minimum MSE** in the causal case:

$$J_{\min}^{\text{causal}} = \frac{1}{2\pi j}\oint \left[S_{dd}(z) - \frac{S_{dx}(z)\,S_{xd}(z)}{S_{xx}(z)}\right]\frac{dz}{z} - J_{\text{excess}}$$

where $J_{\text{excess}}$ is the additional error introduced by causality constraint.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the causal and non-causal Wiener filters. The non-causal filter is a simple ratio $S_{dx}/S_{xx}$; the causal filter requires **spectral factorization** and a partial fraction decomposition that retains only the causal part.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Linear Optimum Filtering System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["Input x(n)"] --> nodeTDL["Tap Delay Line<br/>x(n), x(n-1), ..., x(n-M)"]
    nodeTDL --> nodeMul["Weighted Sum<br/>y(n) = wT x(n)"]
    nodeW["Coefficient Vector w"] --> nodeMul
    nodeMul --> nodeSub["Subtractor<br/>e(n) = d(n) - y(n)"]
    nodeD["Desired Signal d(n)"] --> nodeSub
    nodeSub --> nodeE["Error Signal e(n)"]
    nodeE --> nodeCost["MSE Computer<br/>J = E{ |e(n)| 2 }"]
    nodeCost --> nodeMin{"Minimum?"}
    nodeMin -- No --> nodeAdj["Adjust w<br/>(Theoretical: w_o = R^-1 p)"]
    nodeAdj --> nodeW
    nodeMin -- Yes --> nodeOpt["Optimum w_o<br/>J = J_min"]
    nodeOpt --> nodeOut["Optimum Filtered Output y_o(n)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The input $x(n)$ enters a tapped delay line. Each tap is multiplied by a corresponding weight $w_k$ and summed to produce the filter output $y(n)$. The error $e(n)$ is computed as the difference between the desired signal $d(n)$ and the output. In the **theoretical (non-adaptive) optimum Wiener filter**, the weights are computed *offline* using $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$. In the **adaptive** version, the weights are iteratively adjusted to converge to $\mathbf{w}_o$.

---

## 4.2 Geometry of the Error Performance Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Weight Space w = w0, w1"] --> nodeB["Quadratic MSE Surface<br/>J(w) = paraboloid"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Contours of Constant J<br/>(ellipses in 2D, hyper-ellipsoids in higher dimensions)"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Global Minimum<br/>J_min at w_o"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Eigenvectors of R<br/>= Principal Axes of Ellipses"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Direction of Steepest Descent<br/>= -gradient of J"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Curvature in direction i<br/>= 2 * lambda_i"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The contour lines of constant MSE are **ellipses** (or hyper-ellipsoids in higher dimensions) centered at $\mathbf{w}_o$. The principal axes of these ellipses are aligned with the **eigenvectors** of $\mathbf{R}$, and the squared semi-axis lengths are inversely proportional to the **eigenvalues**. A large ratio $\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ produces highly elongated ellipses, making gradient descent converge slowly along the minor axis direction.

---

## 4.3 Orthogonality Principle — Geometric Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDvec["Desired Vector d(n)"] --> nodeErrVec["Error Vector e_o(n)"]
    nodeErrVec --> nodeOrth{"Orthogonality Test<br/>E{e_o x} = 0"}
    nodeXvec["Input Subspace<br/>span of x(n), x(n-1), ... , x(n-M)"] --> nodeYvec["Filter Output Projection<br/>y_o(n) = w_oT x(n)"]
    nodeYvec --> nodeOrth
    nodeOrth -- Perpendicular --> nodeMin["Minimum Distance<br/>J_min = || e_o || 2"]
    nodeErrVec -. Orthogonal .-> nodeXvec
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** The error vector $e_o(n)$ at the optimum is the *shortest distance* from the desired signal $d(n)$ to the input subspace. By the projection theorem in Hilbert space theory, this shortest distance is achieved **if and only if** the error is orthogonal to every vector in the subspace — exactly the orthogonality principle.

---

## 4.4 Comparison: FIR vs IIR Wiener Filter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FIR_Block["FIR WIENER FILTER"]
        direction LR
        f1["x(n)"] --> f2["TDL M+1 taps"] --> f3["Linear Combiner wT x"] --> f4["yFIR(n)"]
    end

    subgraph IIR_Block["IIR WIENER FILTER"]
        direction LR
        i1["x(n)"] --> i2["Pole Zero Filter H(z)"] --> i3["All Pole A z Part"] --> i4["All Zero B z Part"] --> i5["yIIR(n)"]
    end

    FIR_Block --> FIR_OUT["J_min_FIR = sigma_d 2 - pT R-1 p"]
    IIR_Block --> IIR_OUT["J_min_IIR via Spectral Factorization"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT752]`

**State and explain the orthogonality principle as applied to linear optimum filtering.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement:** *At the optimum Wiener filter setting, the estimation error $e_o(n)$ is statistically uncorrelated (orthogonal) with every tap input in the filter structure.*
>
> **Mathematical Form:**
>
> $$E\{e_o(n)\, x(n-k)\} = 0, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M$$
>
> Equivalently in vector form:
>
> $$E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{0}$$
>
> **Significance (1 mark):** This is the *necessary and sufficient condition* for optimality. It implies that the optimum filter output $y_o(n) = \mathbf{w}_o^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ is the **orthogonal projection** of the desired signal $d(n)$ onto the subspace spanned by the input samples. Geometrically, the error vector is perpendicular to the input subspace, achieving the minimum possible length (i.e., minimum MSE).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PERAT752]`

**Derive the matrix form of the Wiener-Hopf equation for an FIR optimum filter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The MSE cost function is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R}\, \mathbf{w}$$

Taking the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$$

Setting $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \mathbf{0}$ at the optimum:

$$\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

This matrix equation is called the **Wiener-Hopf equation**, where $\mathbf{R}$ is the autocorrelation matrix and $\mathbf{p}$ is the cross-correlation vector between $d(n)$ and the input.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks on this topic due to three pitfalls:
> 1. **Confusing transpose and Hermitian** in complex-signal problems.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify positive definiteness** of $\mathbf{R}$ before claiming invertibility.
> 3. **Skipping the orthogonality derivation** and going directly to $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ — this costs at least 2–3 marks as the orthogonality principle is the *conceptual heart* of the topic.
> 4. **Missing the units/dimensions** of $\mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{p}$, $\mathbf{w}_o$ — always state them explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT752]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the mean square error performance surface $J(\mathbf{w})$ of an FIR linear optimum filter in terms of the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ and cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$. Show that the cost function is a quadratic function of the filter coefficients. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Starting from the quadratic MSE expression, derive the Wiener-Hopf equation for the optimum filter coefficients. State the orthogonality principle and show that it is a direct consequence of the optimum condition. Compute $\mathbf{w}_o$ and $J_{\min}$ for the case:

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \sigma_d^2 = 5$$

**\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation of the MSE Performance Surface [7 Marks]**

The filter output is $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} w_k\,x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$.

The estimation error is:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$$

The mean-square error is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

Expanding:

$$J = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2\mathbf{w}^T E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\} + E\{\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\mathbf{w}\}$$

Since $\mathbf{w}$ is deterministic:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$$

where:

- $\sigma_d^2 = E\{d^2(n)\}$ — variance of $d(n)$.
- $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ — cross-correlation vector.
- $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$ — autocorrelation matrix.

**Quadratic in $\mathbf{w}$:** Since $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric positive-definite (assuming WSS persistent excitation), $J(\mathbf{w})$ is a strict convex quadratic function of $\mathbf{w}$, guaranteeing a unique global minimum.

**Q.E.D. (7 marks)**

**(b) Wiener-Hopf equation and numerical computation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Compute gradient:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0} \Longrightarrow \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

**Orthogonality Principle:** Differentiating the scalar form $E\{e_o(n)\,x(n-k)\} = 0$, $k = 0,\ldots,M$, or in vector form:

$$E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{0}$$

Substituting $e_o(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}_o^T\mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}_o^T\mathbf{x}(n)]\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{0}$$

$$\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{w}_o^T\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{0}^T \Longrightarrow \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$$

Hence the orthogonality principle and the Wiener-Hopf equation are equivalent.

**Step 2: Numerical Computation**

$$\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \frac{1}{(2)(2) - (1)(1)}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{w}_o = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 - 2 \\ -1 + 4 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Minimum MSE**

$$J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w}_o = 5 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 5 - 2 = 3$$

Therefore $\mathbf{w}_o = [0,\ 1]^T$ and $J_{\min} = 3$ (in normalized units).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PERAT752]`

**(a)** Define the linear optimum filtering problem. With the help of a block diagram, explain the transversal FIR Wiener filter and the role of each block. Define the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ and cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$ explicitly with reference to the autocorrelation sequence $r_x(k)$ and cross-correlation sequence $r_{dx}(k)$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** State and prove the orthogonality principle. Show that it leads to the discrete-time Wiener-Hopf equation. Discuss the geometric meaning of the principle in terms of projection in Hilbert space and explain why it implies that the optimum filter achieves minimum MSE. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Linear Optimum Filtering Problem and Block Diagram [7 Marks]**

**Definition:** Linear optimum filtering is the statistical design of a linear discrete-time filter whose coefficients are chosen to minimize the mean-square value of the estimation error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ between a desired response $d(n)$ and the filter output $y(n)$. The "optimum" is in the **MMSE (Minimum Mean Square Error)** sense.

**Block Diagram Description** *(refer to Section 4.1)*:

1. **Tap Delay Line (TDL):** Stores $M+1$ most recent input samples $\{x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M)\}$.
2. **Multipliers:** Scale each tap by its corresponding weight $w_k$.
3. **Adder:** Sums all weighted taps to produce $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$.
4. **Subtractor:** Computes error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
5. **MSE Computer (theoretical):** Computes $J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\}$ using known second-order statistics.

**Autocorrelation Matrix $\mathbf{R}$:**

$$\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\} = \begin{bmatrix} r_x(0) & r_x(1) & \cdots & r_x(M) \\ r_x(1) & r_x(0) & \cdots & r_x(M-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_x(M) & r_x(M-1) & \cdots & r_x(0) \end{bmatrix}$$

Note that $R_{ij} = r_x(\vert i-j \vert)$ — Toeplitz structure.

**Cross-correlation Vector $\mathbf{p}$:**

$$\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{dx}(0) \\ r_{dx}(1) \\ \vdots \\ r_{dx}(M) \end{bmatrix}$$

where $r_{dx}(k) = E\{d(n)\,x(n-k)\}$ is the cross-correlation sequence.

**(b) Orthogonality Principle — Statement and Proof [7 Marks]**

**Statement:** At the optimum Wiener solution, the error $e_o(n)$ is uncorrelated with every input sample used by the filter:

$$E\{e_o(n)\,x(n-k)\} = 0 \quad \text{for all } k = 0, 1, \ldots, M$$

**Proof:**

*Step 1:* Express the error as $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$.

*Step 2:* Consider the scalar function $J_k = E\{[d(n) - w_k\,x(n-k)]^2\}$ (treating one tap at a time). Differentiating with respect to $w_k$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial w_k} = -2 E\{d(n)\,x(n-k)\} + 2 w_k E\{x^2(n-k)\} = 0$$

Extending to all taps jointly:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

*Step 3:* Since $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ minimizes $J$, at the optimum:

$$E\{e_o(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}_o^T\mathbf{x}(n)]\mathbf{x}(n)\} = \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{0}$$

This is the orthogonality condition. Each component $E\{e_o(n)\,x(n-k)\} = 0$ independently.

**Geometric Interpretation:** In the Hilbert space of random variables with inner product $\langle u, v \rangle = E\{u\,v\}$, the space of tap inputs $\{x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M)\}$ defines an $M+1$ dimensional subspace. The optimum filter output $y_o(n) = \mathbf{w}_o^T\mathbf{x}(n)$ is the **orthogonal projection** of $d(n)$ onto this subspace. The error $e_o(n)$ is the **residual component orthogonal to the subspace**, and by the Pythagorean theorem in Hilbert space, this is the shortest distance from $d(n)$ to the subspace — i.e., minimum MSE.

**Why Minimum MSE?** Since $e_o(n) \perp \mathbf{x}(n)$, expanding $J$:

$$J = E\{(y_o(n) + e_o(n))^2\} = E\{y_o^2(n)\} + 2 E\{y_o(n)\,e_o(n)\} + E\{e_o^2(n)\}$$

Because $y_o(n) = \mathbf{w}_o^T\mathbf{x}(n)$ is a linear combination of $\mathbf{x}(n)$ and is orthogonal to $e_o(n)$, the cross-term vanishes:

$$J = E\{y_o^2(n)\} + E\{e_o^2(n)\} = \mathbf{w}_o^T\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o + J_{\min}$$

Since $E\{y_o^2(n)\} \geq 0$, the MSE is minimized when this term is *maximized*, which happens exactly when the filter weights equal the projection coefficients $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Optimum Filtering**

- [x] **Filter output:** $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$ — a linear combination of tap inputs.
- [x] **Error signal:** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ — difference between desired and actual.
- [x] **Cost function:** $J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$ — **quadratic** in $\mathbf{w}$.
- [x] **Autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$:** Symmetric, Toeplitz, positive-definite (under persistent excitation).
- [x] **Cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$:** Cross-correlation $r_{dx}(k) = E\{d(n)x(n-k)\}$.
- [x] **Wiener-Hopf equation:** $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$ — matrix normal equation.
- [x] **Optimum weights:** $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ — unique global solution.
- [x] **Orthogonality principle:** $E\{e_o(n)\,x(n-k)\} = 0$ for all $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M$.
- [x] **Minimum MSE:** $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ — irreducible estimation error.
- [x] **Error performance surface:** Convex hyperparaboloid with unique global minimum at $\mathbf{w}_o$.
- [x] **Non-causal IIR Wiener filter:** $H_{\text{nc}}(z) = S_{dx}(z)/S_{xx}(z)$.
- [x] **Causal IIR Wiener filter:** Requires spectral factorization $S_{xx}(z) = S_{xx}^{+}(z) S_{xx}^{-}(z)$.
- [x] **Complex extension:** Replace $T$ with $H$ (Hermitian transpose).
- [x] **Hilbert space view:** Optimum output = orthogonal projection of $d(n)$ onto input subspace.
- [x] **Practical limitation:** Requires knowledge of $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$ — motivates *adaptive* algorithms (LMS, RLS).

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Principle of Orthogonality** is a fundamental statistical theorem in adaptive signal processing which states that:

> When an adaptive linear estimator (such as the Wiener filter) operates at its **optimum point** (i.e., achieves the **Minimum Mean Square Error - MMSE**), the estimation error sequence $e(n)$ is **mutually orthogonal** (uncorrelated) to every input sample $x(n-k)$ used in forming the estimate.

Mathematically, for an FIR adaptive filter of order $p$, the orthogonality condition is expressed as:

$$
E[e(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, p-1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight for KTU 2024 Scheme:** This principle is the **cornerstone derivation tool** for the Wiener-Hopf equation $R\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{r}$, and forms the mathematical justification for the convergence behavior of the LMS algorithm. It is a **CO2 / CO3 level topic** testing the *Application* and *Analysis* cognitive levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Think of the Principle of Orthogonality like **throwing a dart at a dartboard** in 3D space:

- Imagine the **dartboard** is a plane made of stretched canvas (this represents the **subspace spanned by the input signal samples** $x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-p+1)$).
- The **dart** you throw represents the **desired signal** $d(n)$.
- Your **projection** (shadow) on the canvas is the **best estimate** $\hat{d}(n) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$.
- The **perpendicular distance** from the dart tip to the canvas is the **error** $e(n)$.

**The "aha" moment:** The *shortest* possible distance from the dart to the canvas is always a **perpendicular (orthogonal) drop**. Any other angle would give a longer, slanted distance. Similarly, the MMSE filter is the one that makes the error $e(n)$ stand *perpendicular* to every input vector that forms the estimation plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Example:** In **noise-cancelling headphones**, the adaptive filter must produce an anti-noise signal that is orthogonal to the residual error in the ear canal at the optimum. The moment error and reference noise become orthogonal, the filter has converged and noise cancellation is maximized.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities (Bolded)

| Quantity | Symbol | Units / Nature |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| Estimation error | $e(n)$ | Volts (V) or arbitrary amplitude units |
| Input signal | $x(n)$ | Volts (V) |
| Desired/Primary signal | $d(n)$ | Volts (V) |
| Filter order | $p$ | Dimensionless integer |
| Autocorrelation matrix | $R$ | Power units ($\text{V}^2$) |
| Cross-correlation vector | $\mathbf{r}$ | Power units ($\text{V}^2$) |
| Mean Square Error | $J_{\min}$ | Power units ($\text{V}^2$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Assumption:** All signals are assumed to be **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** and **zero-mean**, with finite second-order moments so that expectations and correlations are well-defined.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theorem — Broken Down into Logic Steps

The Principle of Orthogonality is not an assumption — it is a **derivable consequence** of minimizing the Mean Square Error (MSE) cost function. The logical flow is as follows:

### Step 1: Define the Linear Estimator
A linear transversal (tapped-delay-line) FIR filter produces an estimate $\hat{d}(n)$ of the desired response $d(n)$ using $p$ past input samples:

$$
\hat{d}(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} w_k(n)\,x(n-k)
$$

### Step 2: Form the Estimation Error
The error (innovation) is the difference between the desired signal and its estimate:

$$
e(n) = d(n) - \hat{d}(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

### Step 3: Construct the MSE Cost Function
The mean square error is the expected value of the squared error:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\,e^2(n)\,] = E[\,(\,d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\,)^2\,]
$$

### Step 4: Differentiate with Respect to the Weight Vector
The gradient of the MSE with respect to the weights gives the steepest ascent direction. For minimum $J$, this gradient must vanish:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2\,E[\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = 0
$$

### Step 5: Equate the Gradient to Zero Vector
Setting the gradient equal to the zero vector yields:

$$
E[\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = \mathbf{0} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad E[\,e(n)\,x(n-k)\,] = 0
$$

This is precisely the **orthogonality condition** — the error is decorrelated (orthogonal) from every input sample in the tap-delay line.

### Step 6: Expand the Expectation to Derive the Wiener-Hopf Equation
Substituting $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ into the orthogonality condition:

$$
E[\,(d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n))\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = \mathbf{0}
$$

This expands to the famous **Wiener-Hopf equation**:

$$
R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}
$$

where $R = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)]$ is the $p \times p$ autocorrelation matrix and $\mathbf{r} = E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$ is the $p \times 1$ cross-correlation vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The optimum weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ is the unique projection of the desired signal vector $d(n)$ onto the subspace spanned by the input vectors. The error is the orthogonal "leftover" component that cannot be removed by any linear combination of the inputs.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning** |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Orthogonality Condition | $E[e(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0, \quad k = 0, \ldots, p-1$ | Error uncorrelated with every input tap |
| Error Definition | $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Innovation / residual |
| MSE Cost Function | $J = E[e^2(n)]$ | Performance measure to be minimized |
| Optimum Gradient | $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$ | First-order necessary optimality condition |
| Wiener-Hopf Equation | $R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}$ | Closed-form optimum solution |
| Autocorrelation Matrix | $R = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)]$, $R_{ij} = r_x(i-j)$ | Toeplitz, symmetric, positive-definite |
| Cross-Correlation Vector | $\mathbf{r} = E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$ | Input-desired correlation |
| Minimum MSE | $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{r}$ | Residual error power at optimum |
| Correlation Property of Error | $E[e(n)\,d(n)] = J_{min}$ | Error orthogonal to estimate (only to estimate, not to desired) |
| Positive Semi-Definiteness | $\mathbf{a}^T R \mathbf{a} \geq 0 \quad \forall\, \mathbf{a} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Guarantees unique global minimum |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Students often write $E[e(n)\,d(n)] = 0$. This is **FALSE**. The error is orthogonal to the *input samples* $x(n-k)$, not to the *desired signal* $d(n)$. The correct relation is $E[e(n)\,\hat{d}(n)] = 0$, since the error is orthogonal to its own linear estimate.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The orthogonality principle is the unifying thread that connects multiple adaptive filtering applications:

1. **System Identification:** Modeling an unknown plant — when the error (difference between plant output and adaptive model output) becomes orthogonal to the input, the model has converged.
2. **Noise Cancellation (ANC):** In active noise control, the anti-noise signal is optimal precisely when the residual error in the error microphone is orthogonal to the reference noise.
3. **Channel Equalization:** The equalizer converges when the intersymbol interference (ISI) component of the error is orthogonal to the received symbols.
4. **Adaptive Beamforming:** The beamformer output is MMSE-optimal when its error is orthogonal to the array snapshot vector.
5. **Echo Cancellation:** In VoIP and hands-free telephony, the echo canceller converges when the residual echo becomes orthogonal to the far-end speech used as reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Relevance:** The orthogonality principle directly underpins the derivation of the **LMS algorithm** (Module 3), the **RLS algorithm** (Module 4), and the analysis of **convergence behavior** (Module 5) — making it a *gateway concept* for the rest of the course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Orthogonality Principle from MSE Minimization

We begin with the **mean square error** as a scalar cost function of the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\,e^2(n)\,] = E[\,(\,d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\,)^2\,]
$$

**Step 1: Expand the squared term inside the expectation operator.**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\,d^2(n)\,] - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T E[d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)] + \mathbf{w}^T E[\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)]\,\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the correlation terms using standard notation.**

Define the variance of the desired signal:

$$
\sigma_d^2 = E[\,d^2(n)\,]
$$

Define the $p \times 1$ cross-correlation vector:

$$
\mathbf{r} = E[\,d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = \begin{bmatrix} E[d(n)\,x(n)] \\ E[d(n)\,x(n-1)] \\ \vdots \\ E[d(n)\,x(n-p+1)] \end{bmatrix}
$$

Define the $p \times p$ autocorrelation matrix:

$$
R = E[\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\,] = \begin{bmatrix} r_x(0) & r_x(1) & \cdots & r_x(p-1) \\ r_x(1) & r_x(0) & \cdots & r_x(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_x(p-1) & r_x(p-2) & \cdots & r_x(0) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Rewrite $J(\mathbf{w})$ in compact matrix form.**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{w}^T R\,\mathbf{w}
$$

This is a **quadratic function** of $\mathbf{w}$. Since $R$ is positive-definite (for persistently exciting inputs), $J(\mathbf{w})$ is a **convex bowl** with a unique global minimum.

**Step 4: Take the gradient of $J(\mathbf{w})$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$.**

Using the matrix differentiation rules $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{r}) = \mathbf{r}$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^T R \mathbf{w}) = 2R\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\,\mathbf{r} + 2\,R\,\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 5: Set the gradient to zero for the optimum.**

$$
-2\,\mathbf{r} + 2\,R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 6: Re-derive the orthogonality condition directly from $R\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}$.**

Multiply the error $e_{opt}(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ by $\mathbf{x}(n)$ and take the expectation:

$$
E[\,e_{opt}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = E[d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)] - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T E[\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)]
$$

$$
= \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T R = \mathbf{r} - (R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt})^T = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}^T = \mathbf{0}
$$

(Here we used the fact that $R$ is symmetric so that $(R\mathbf{w}_{opt})^T = \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T R$.)

Thus the orthogonality principle is **proved** as a necessary and sufficient condition for the MSE optimum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Mean Square Error $J_{min}$

**Step 1:** Start from the expression of $J$ at the optimum weight vector:

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}$ into the last two terms:

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r}
$$

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R^{-1} \mathbf{r}$:

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - (R^{-1}\mathbf{r})^T \mathbf{r} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{r}
$$

This is the **irreducible error floor** that determines the best achievable performance of any linear filter on the given data statistics.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component-wise Form of Orthogonality

For a 2-tap filter ($p = 2$), the orthogonality condition $E[e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$ expands to two scalar equations:

$$
E[\,e(n)\,x(n)\,] = 0 \quad \text{(Equation 1)}
$$

$$
E[\,e(n)\,x(n-1)\,] = 0 \quad \text{(Equation 2)}
$$

Substituting $e(n) = d(n) - w_0 x(n) - w_1 x(n-1)$ into Equation 1:

$$
E[\,d(n)\,x(n)\,] - w_0 E[x^2(n)] - w_1 E[x(n)x(n-1)] = 0
$$

$$
r_{dx}(0) - w_0 r_x(0) - w_1 r_x(1) = 0
$$

Similarly for Equation 2:

$$
r_{dx}(1) - w_0 r_x(1) - w_1 r_x(0) = 0
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} r_x(0) & r_x(1) \\ r_x(1) & r_x(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{dx}(0) \\ r_{dx}(1) \end{bmatrix}
$$

This $R\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{r}$ formulation is the **Wiener-Hopf equation** for $p=2$, and directly follows from the orthogonality principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The matrix $R$ is **Toeplitz** (constant along each diagonal) and **symmetric positive-definite**. Toeplitz structure arises because $R_{ij} = r_x(i-j)$ depends only on the lag, and positive-definiteness is guaranteed for non-zero persistently exciting inputs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Vector Interpretation of Orthogonality (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputX["Input Vector x of dimension p"]
    DesiredD["Desired Signal d of dimension N"]
    Subspace["Input Subspace span of x n x n-1 ... x n-p+1"]
    Estimate["Optimum Estimate d-hat equals w-opt transpose x"]
    ErrorVec["Error Vector e equals d minus d-hat"]
    OrthoCheck["Orthogonality Check E of e times x equals 0"]
    WienerEq["Wiener Hopf Equation R w-opt equals r"]
    Jmin["Minimum MSE J-min equals sigma-d squared minus r transpose R inverse r"]

    InputX --> Subspace
    DesiredD --> Estimate
    Subspace --> Estimate
    DesiredD --> ErrorVec
    Estimate --> ErrorVec
    ErrorVec --> OrthoCheck
    OrthoCheck -->|condition satisfied| WienerEq
    WienerEq --> Jmin
```

## 4.2 Adaptive Filter Block Diagram (Mermaid Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xn["x of n"] --> Delay1["Delay z to power minus 1"]
    Delay1 --> Delay2["Delay z to power minus 1"]
    Delay2 --> DelayPminus1["Delay z to power minus 1 up to p-1 taps"]
    xn --> W0["Multiplier w-0"]
    Delay1 --> W1["Multiplier w-1"]
    Delay2 --> W2["Multiplier w-2"]
    DelayPminus1 --> Wpminus1["Multiplier w sub p-1"]
    W0 --> SumJunction["Adder Junction"]
    W1 --> SumJunction
    W2 --> SumJunction
    Wpminus1 --> SumJunction
    SumJunction --> Estimate["Estimate d-hat of n"]
    dn["Desired d of n"] --> ErrorSub["Subtractor"]
    Estimate --> ErrorSub
    ErrorSub --> En["Error e of n"]
    En -->|E e times x equals 0| OrthogonalityMarker["Orthogonality Condition Satisfied"]
    OrthogonalityMarker -->|minimize J| AdaptationEngine["Weight Adaptation Engine"]
    AdaptationEngine --> W0
    AdaptationEngine --> W1
    AdaptationEngine --> W2
    AdaptationEngine --> Wpminus1
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow of MSE Minimization to Orthogonality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Define Linear Estimator d-hat equals w-transpose x"]
    ErrorDef["Form Error e equals d minus d-hat"]
    MSEDef["Define MSE Cost J equals E of e squared"]
    Gradient["Compute Gradient of J with respect to w"]
    OptCond["Set Gradient to Zero Vector"]
    Ortho["Obtain Orthogonality E of e times x equals 0"]
    Wiener["Derive Wiener-Hopf R w-opt equals r"]
    JminCalc["Compute J-min equals sigma-d squared minus r-transpose R-inverse r"]
    Converge["Filter has Reached MMSE Optimum"]

    Start --> ErrorDef
    ErrorDef --> MSEDef
    MSEDef --> Gradient
    Gradient --> OptCond
    OptCond --> Ortho
    Ortho --> Wiener
    Wiener --> JminCalc
    JminCalc --> Converge
```

> [!NOTE]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam - April 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q1.** State and explain the **Principle of Orthogonality** in the context of adaptive linear estimation. Why is it called the *necessary and sufficient* condition for MMSE?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Principle of Orthogonality states that when an adaptive linear filter operating on a stationary stochastic input attains its **minimum mean square error (MMSE)**, the estimation error $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ becomes **orthogonal** (uncorrelated) to every input sample $x(n-k)$ in the tap-delay line used for estimation, i.e., $E[e(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, p-1$. It is called a *necessary and sufficient* condition because the gradient of the MSE cost function $J = E[e^2(n)]$ with respect to each weight is exactly $-2E[e(n)x(n-k)]$, so setting the gradient to zero is equivalent to demanding the orthogonality condition. Conversely, if orthogonality holds for all $p$ taps and the autocorrelation matrix $R$ is non-singular, then $\mathbf{w}$ must be the unique MMSE solution. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: Short Analytical
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank]**

> **Q2.** An FIR Wiener filter of order $p = 2$ operates on a stationary process with autocorrelation $r_x(0) = 4$, $r_x(1) = 2$ and cross-correlation $r_{dx}(0) = 3$, $r_{dx}(1) = 1$. Apply the orthogonality principle to set up the Wiener-Hopf equations in matrix form.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Wiener-Hopf equation $R \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}$ in component form requires:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} r_x(0) & r_x(1) \\ r_x(1) & r_x(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{dx}(0) \\ r_{dx}(1) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the direct matrix manifestation of the orthogonality principle for $p = 2$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - End Semester Pattern: Internal Choice Provided]**

### CHOICE — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **Q(A).** Derive the **Wiener-Hopf equation** by minimizing the mean square error of a $p$-tap FIR adaptive filter. Show clearly how the **principle of orthogonality** emerges as a natural consequence of this minimization. Also compute the **minimum mean square error** $J_{min}$ in terms of $\sigma_d^2$, $\mathbf{r}$, and $R$.

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Orthogonality Principle (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the linear estimate.** For a $p$-tap FIR filter with weight vector $\mathbf{w} = [w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{p-1}]^T$ and tap input $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-p+1)]^T$:

$$
\hat{d}(n) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)
$$

**Step 2: Form the estimation error:**

$$
e(n) = d(n) - \hat{d}(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)
$$

**Step 3: Formulate the MSE cost function:**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\,e^2(n)\,] = E[\,(\,d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\,)^2\,]
$$

**Step 4: Expand the expectation:**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{w}^T R\,\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 5: Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and equate to zero:**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\,\mathbf{r} + 2\,R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 6: Reinterpret as orthogonality.** Multiply the error by $\mathbf{x}(n)$ and take expectation:

$$
E[\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = \mathbf{r} - R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Hence, **$E[e(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, p-1$** is the **Principle of Orthogonality**. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Derivation of $J_{min}$ (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Express $J$ at the optimum weight vector:**

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt}
$$

**Step 2: Use $R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}$ to simplify the last two terms:**

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R^{-1}\mathbf{r}$:**

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - (R^{-1}\mathbf{r})^T \mathbf{r} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{r}
$$

**Geometric Remark:** This is the *irreducible error power* — the variance of $d(n)$ that cannot be removed by any linear combination of the inputs. The term $\mathbf{r}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{r}$ represents the *variance explained* by the optimal linear filter. **(7 marks)**

---

### CHOICE — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

> **Q(B).** With the help of a suitable **block diagram**, explain the operation of a $p$-tap adaptive transversal filter. Starting from the **orthogonality condition** $E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$, derive the **Wiener-Hopf equation** and discuss the **geometric interpretation** of the optimum solution.

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Working (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

A $p$-tap adaptive transversal filter (see Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.2) consists of:
1. A **tapped delay line** with $p$ unit-delay elements producing $x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-p+1)$.
2. **$p$ multipliers** scaling each tap by weights $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{p-1}$.
3. A **summing junction** producing the estimate $\hat{d}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} w_k x(n-k)$.
4. A **subtractor** forming the error $e(n) = d(n) - \hat{d}(n)$.
5. An **adaptation engine** that adjusts $\mathbf{w}$ to drive $E[e^2(n)]$ to its minimum.

The filter operates in real time, continuously updating its weights to minimize the error power. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Wiener-Hopf from Orthogonality (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Start from the orthogonality condition** $E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
E[\,(\,d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\,)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 2: Apply linearity of expectation:**

$$
E[\,d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] - E[\,\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 3: Factor out the deterministic weight vector $\mathbf{w}$:**

$$
E[\,d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,] - \mathbf{w}^T E[\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\,] = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 4: Identify the two correlation terms:**

$$
\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{w}^T R = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 5: Transpose both sides and use symmetry of $R$:**

$$
R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}
$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** The optimum weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ defines a *projection operator* onto the subspace spanned by the input vectors. The error $e_{opt}(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ is the *residual* component of $d(n)$ that lies *orthogonal* to this subspace — no further reduction in $E[e^2(n)]$ is possible by any linear filter. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points on Orthogonality Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Gradient:** Forgetting the factor of **$-2$** in $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$ — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Correlation Targets:** Writing $E[e(n)\,d(n)] = 0$ instead of $E[e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)] = 0$. The error is orthogonal to the **inputs**, **not** to the **desired signal**.
> 3. **Skipping the Stationarity Assumption:** Without WSS, the autocorrelation matrix $R$ and cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{r}$ are not time-invariant and the derivation collapses. Always state the assumption.
> 4. **Forgetting Matrix Symmetry:** Using $R^T$ vs. $R$ interchangeably without justification. State that $R$ is symmetric explicitly.
> 5. **Inverting a Singular $R$:** The autocorrelation matrix must be **positive-definite** (i.e., the input must be *persistently exciting*) for $R^{-1}$ to exist. Mention this condition explicitly.
> 6. **Confusing the Estimate and the Desired Signal:** $E[e(n)\hat{d}(n)] = 0$ is **true**, but $E[e(n)d(n)] = J_{min}$ (not zero). Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Orthogonality Principle:** $E[e(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, p-1$ at MMSE optimum.
- ✅ **Origin:** Derived from setting $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \mathbf{0}$ where $J = E[e^2(n)]$.
- ✅ **Equivalence to Wiener-Hopf:** $R\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}$ and orthogonality are *two sides of the same coin*.
- ✅ **Geometric Meaning:** The error is perpendicular to the input subspace; the estimate is the orthogonal projection of $d(n)$ onto that subspace.
- ✅ **Statistics Required:** Signals must be **WSS**, **zero-mean**, and the input must be **persistently exciting** ($R$ positive-definite).
- ✅ **Minimum MSE:** $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{r} \geq 0$, achievable only when $d(n)$ lies wholly in the input subspace.
- ✅ **Key Identity:** $E[e_{opt}(n)\,\hat{d}(n)] = 0$ — error is orthogonal to its own estimate.
- ✅ **Application Anchor:** Orthogonality is the **theoretical justification** for the convergence of LMS and RLS adaptive algorithms.
- ✅ **Matrix Property:** $R$ is **Toeplitz** (constant diagonals) and **symmetric positive-definite** (real inputs, persistently exciting).
- ✅ **Pitfall:** Do not confuse "orthogonal to inputs" with "orthogonal to desired signal" — these are *not* the same.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT752 - ADAPTIVE SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Mean Square Error

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE)** criterion is a fundamental optimization principle in adaptive signal processing. It states that the *optimum* set of filter coefficients (weights) is the one that **minimizes the expected value of the squared error signal** between the desired response and the actual filter output.

Mathematically, the MMSE criterion is expressed as:

$$J_{\min} = \min_{\mathbf{w}} \; E\left[ \vert e(n) \vert^2 \right]$$

where $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ is the error signal, $d(n)$ is the desired response, $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ is the filter output, and $\mathbf{w}$ is the weight vector of the adaptive filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The solution to the MMSE problem yields the **Wiener-Hopf equations**, and the corresponding filter is known as the **Wiener Filter**. This forms the theoretical foundation for virtually all adaptive algorithms (LMS, RLS, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a DJ in a noisy concert hall trying to match the *true* music signal (the "desired" signal) using a microphone that picks up both music and crowd noise.

- You have a set of **volume sliders** (the filter weights $\mathbf{w}$).
- Your **goal** is to reduce the *loudness of the error* (the difference between the original music and your amplified version) to its **lowest possible average value**.
- The MMSE criterion tells you: *"Adjust the sliders so that the average squared error is the smallest it can ever be."*

In simple terms, MMSE finds the **single best "average" setting** of the filter — not a setting that works perfectly for one moment, but the one that *statistically* gives the smallest possible error over time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **MMSE (Wiener Solution):** Closed-form, batch, requires second-order statistics ($\mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{P}$).
> - **Adaptive Algorithms (LMS/RLS):** Iterative, sample-by-sample, estimates the Wiener solution online.

### 1.3 Physical Significance of the Cost Function

The mean square error (MSE) is chosen as the cost function $J(\mathbf{w})$ because:

1. It is a **mathematically tractable** quadratic function of $\mathbf{w}$.
2. It leads to a **unique global minimum** (unimodal surface).
3. It is **physically meaningful** — it corresponds to signal *power*, which engineers can measure and interpret.
4. Differentiating $E[e^2(n)]$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ yields a **linear system of equations** (Wiener-Hopf equations) — easy to solve.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Board Exam:** "MMSE = **M**ake **M**ean **S**quared **E**rror **M**inimal" — and the recipe is **Wiener-Hopf Equation**: $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{P}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MMSE Optimization Problem — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define the Filter Output (Linear Transversal Filter)**

A linear transversal (FIR) filter of order $N$ produces:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k \, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$$

where the tap-input vector and weight vector are:

$$\mathbf{x}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} x(n) \\ x(n-1) \\ \vdots \\ x(n-N+1) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \\ \vdots \\ w_{N-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Error Signal**

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the Cost Function (Mean Square Error)**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\left[ e^2(n) \right] = E\left[ \left( d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n) \right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Cost Function**

Expanding the square:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\left[ d^2(n) \right] - 2 \, \mathbf{w}^T E\left[ d(n) \mathbf{x}(n) \right] + \mathbf{w}^T E\left[ \mathbf{x}(n) \mathbf{x}^T(n) \right] \mathbf{w}$$

This reduces to the famous **quadratic form**:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2 \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)]$ → **Autocorrelation matrix** of the input (size $N \times N$).
- $\mathbf{P} = E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$ → **Cross-correlation vector** between input and desired response (size $N \times 1$).
- $\sigma_d^2 = E[d^2(n)]$ → Variance/power of the desired response.

**Step 5 — Take the Gradient and Set to Zero**
