# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RLS Cost Function — Step-by-Step Logic

The RLS criterion minimises the sum of **squared errors** between the desired response $d(i)$ and the filter output $y(i) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(i)\,\mathbf{x}(i)$, with an **exponential weighting factor** $\lambda^{n-i}$ that emphasises recent samples.

The deterministic least-squares cost function is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\, \big\vert e(i)\big\vert^{2}
$$

where:
- $e(i) = d(i) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(i)$ is the *a posteriori* estimation error.
- $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ is the **forgetting factor** (also called the exponential weighting factor).
- $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n)$ is the $N \times 1$ tap-weight vector to be estimated at instant $n$.

### Logical Steps of the Cost Function Construction

1. **Sample-by-sample squared error**: $|e(i)|^2 = e(i)\,e^*(i)$ is the instantaneous squared error magnitude.
2. **Exponential weighting**: $0 < \lambda < 1$ ensures older samples contribute less. When $\lambda = 1$, the filter has *infinite memory* (standard least squares); when $\lambda < 1$, the *effective memory* is approximately $\frac{1}{1-\lambda}$ samples.
3. **Setting the gradient to zero**: $\nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{w}}^*} \mathcal{J}(n) = 0$ yields the **normal equations**.
4. **Deterministic interpretation**: Unlike LMS where $\mathbb{E}\{|\mathbf{x}(n)|^2\}$ appears, RLS uses the *true* deterministic autocorrelation $\mathbf{R}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{x}(i)\mathbf{x}^H(i)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA TABLE** — Memorise this for direct marks in ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | RLS Cost function | $\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\, \big\vert e(i)\big\vert^{2}$ | Exponentially weighted LS criterion |
| 2 | Filter output | $y(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | With *old* weights |
| 3 | A priori error | $\xi(n) = d(n) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Error before update |
| 4 | A posteriori error | $e(n) = d(n) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Error after update |
| 5 | Deterministic autocorrelation matrix | $\mathbf{R}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{x}(i)\mathbf{x}^H(i)$ | $N \times N$ Hermitian matrix |
| 6 | Cross-correlation vector | $\mathbf{p}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{x}(i)\,d^*(i)$ | $N \times 1$ |
| 7 | Time recursion of $\mathbf{R}(n)$ | $\mathbf{R}(n) = \lambda\,\mathbf{R}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)$ | Key recursion |
| 8 | Time recursion of $\mathbf{p}(n)$ | $\mathbf{p}(n) = \lambda\,\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,d^*(n)$ | Key recursion |
| 9 | Normal equations | $\mathbf{R}(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{p}(n)$ | Optimality condition |
| 10 | Inverse autocorrelation recursion | $\mathbf{P}(n) = \tfrac{1}{\lambda}\big[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big]$ | From Matrix Inversion Lemma |
| 11 | Gain vector | $\mathbf{k}(n) = \dfrac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$ | $\mathbb{C}^{N \times 1}$ |
| 12 | Weight update | $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\xi^*(n)$ | Final recursive equation |
| 13 | Conversion $\xi(n) \to e(n)$ | $e(n) = \xi(n)\big[1 - \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{k}(n)\big]$ | Useful identity |
| 14 | Matrix Inversion Lemma | $(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{u}\mathbf{v}^H)^{-1} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} - \dfrac{\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}\mathbf{v}^H\mathbf{A}^{-1}}{1 + \mathbf{v}^H\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}}$ | Woodbury identity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units / Ranges to remember:**
> - $\lambda$: dimensionless, range $(0, 1]$. Typical KTU problem values: $\lambda = 0.98, 0.99, 0.95$.
> - $N$: filter order (integer, typically $2$–$64$).
> - $\mathbf{P}(n)$: in units of $[\mathbf{R}(n)]^{-1}$, i.e., sample-power inverse.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Echo cancellation** in VoIP and hands-free telephony.
- **Channel equalisation** in high-speed digital communication (e.g., 5G NR receivers, ADSL modems).
- **System identification** in control engineering — identifying unknown plant dynamics.
- **Biomedical signal processing** — adaptive noise cancellation in ECG/EEG recordings.
- **Radar and sonar** — adaptive beamforming where eigenvalue spread is high.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Normal Equations from the RLS Cost Function

Starting from:

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\, e(i)\,e^*(i)
$$

Substituting $e(i) = d(i) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(i)$:

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\, \big[d(i) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(i)\big]\,\big[d^*(i) - \mathbf{x}^H(i)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n)\big]
$$

Expand the product:

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\, \Big[ \big\vert d(i)\big\vert^{2} - d(i)\,\mathbf{x}^H(i)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(i)\,d^*(i) + \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(i)\,\mathbf{x}^H(i)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) \Big]
$$

Recognising $\sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\mathbf{x}(i)\mathbf{x}^H(i) = \mathbf{R}(n)$ and $\sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\mathbf{x}(i)\,d^*(i) = \mathbf{p}(n)$:

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\big\vert d(i)\big\vert^{2} - \mathbf{p}^H(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n) + \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{R}(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n)
$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\hat{\mathbf{w}}^*(n)$ and set to zero (using $\frac{\partial \mathbf{x}^H \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}}{\partial \mathbf{x}^*} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ when $\mathbf{A}$ is Hermitian):

$$
\nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{w}}^*} \mathcal{J}(n) = -\mathbf{p}(n) + \mathbf{R}(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{0}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{R}(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{p}(n)\,}
$$

and the direct (non-recursive) solution is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n)
$$

This direct solution requires an $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ matrix inversion at every instant $n$, which is computationally prohibitive. The RLS algorithm overcomes this via the **Matrix Inversion Lemma**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Matrix Inversion Lemma (Woodbury Identity)

Given a non-singular $N \times N$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ and vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{C}^{N \times 1}$, the identity states:

$$
(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{u}\mathbf{v}^H)^{-1} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} - \frac{\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}\mathbf{v}^H \mathbf{A}^{-1}}{1 + \mathbf{v}^H \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{u}}
$$

This identity is applied at every instant in RLS to compute $\mathbf{P}(n) = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)$ in $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ operations instead of $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recursive Update of $\mathbf{P}(n)$

Using the recursion $\mathbf{R}(n) = \lambda\,\mathbf{R}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)$, identify:

- $\mathbf{A} = \lambda\,\mathbf{R}(n-1) \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)$
- $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{x}(n)$, $\mathbf{v}^H = \mathbf{x}^H(n)$

Applying the Matrix Inversion Lemma:

$$
\mathbf{P}(n) = \big[\lambda\,\mathbf{R}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\big]^{-1}
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \frac{\big(\frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\big(\frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big)}{1 + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\big(\frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}
$$

Simplifying the scalar denominator $1 + \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \frac{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda}$:

$$
\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \frac{\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)}{\frac{1}{\lambda}\big[\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\big]}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator:

$$
\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}\right]
$$

Define the **gain vector**:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}\,}
$$

So the recursive inverse update becomes:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\big[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big]\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Final RLS Weight Update

We have $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n)$. Substitute $\mathbf{p}(n) = \lambda\,\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,d^*(n)$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n)\,\big[\lambda\,\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,d^*(n)\big]
$$

Substitute the expression for $\mathbf{P}(n)$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\big[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big]\,\big[\lambda\,\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,d^*(n)\big]
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,d^*(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{p}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\,\frac{\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda}\,d^*(n)
$$

Note that $\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{p}(n-1) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1)$. After algebraic manipulation (collecting terms involving $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1)$ and the new sample), the result simplifies beautifully:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\big[d^*(n) - \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1)\big]
$$

Recognising the bracket as $\xi^*(n) = d^*(n) - \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1)$:

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\xi^*(n)\,}
$$

## 3.5 Initialisation Conditions

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(0) = \mathbf{0}, \qquad \mathbf{P}(0) = \delta^{-1}\,\mathbf{I}_N
$$

where $\delta$ is a small positive regularisation constant (typically $\delta = 0.01$ or smaller), and $\mathbf{I}_N$ is the $N \times N$ identity matrix. This ensures $\mathbf{R}(0) = \delta\,\mathbf{I}_N$ is invertible.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RLS Adaptive Filter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["Input Vector x(n) — length N"] --> nodeMX["Tap-Weight Inner Product"]
    nodeW["Weight Vector w_hat(n-1)"] --> nodeMX
    nodeMX --> nodeY["Filter Output y(n) = w_hat_H * x(n)"]
    nodeD["Desired Signal d(n)"] --> nodeERR["Error Formulation e_block"]
    nodeY --> nodeERR
    nodeERR --> nodeXI["A priori Error xi(n) = d(n) - y(n)"]
    nodeXI --> nodeK["Gain Vector Update k(n) = P(n-1)*x(n) / (lambda + x_H(n)*P(n-1)*x(n))"]
    nodeP["Inverse Correlation P(n-1)"] --> nodeK
    nodeX --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodePUP["P(n) Update via Matrix Inversion Lemma"]
    nodeP --> nodePUP
    nodePUP --> nodeP
    nodeXI --> nodeWU["Weight Update w_hat(n) = w_hat(n-1) + k(n)*xi_star(n)"]
    nodeK --> nodeWU
    nodeW --> nodeWU
    nodeWU --> nodeW
    nodeERR --> nodeOUT["A posteriori Error e(n)"]
```

## 4.2 RLS Algorithm Flow — Sequential Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["STEP 1 — Initialise w_hat(0) = 0, P(0) = delta_inverse * I"] --> nodeS2
    nodeS2["STEP 2 — Acquire x(n), d(n)"] --> nodeS3
    nodeS3["STEP 3 — Compute filter output y(n) = w_hat_H(n-1) * x(n)"] --> nodeS4
    nodeS4["STEP 4 — Compute a priori error xi(n) = d(n) - y(n)"] --> nodeS5
    nodeS5["STEP 5 — Compute gain vector k(n) = P(n-1)*x(n) / (lambda + x_H(n)*P(n-1)*x(n))"] --> nodeS6
    nodeS6["STEP 6 — Update weight vector w_hat(n) = w_hat(n-1) + k(n)*xi_star(n)"] --> nodeS7
    nodeS7["STEP 7 — Update P(n) = (1/lambda)*[P(n-1) - k(n)*x_H(n)*P(n-1)]"] --> nodeS8
    nodeS8["STEP 8 — Increment n and return to STEP 2"]
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Visualisation of Key Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["x(n) ∈ ℂ^(N×1)"] -- "outer product x(n)*x_H(n)" --> nodeB["R(n) ∈ ℂ^(N×N)"]
    nodeB -- "Inverse via Matrix Inversion Lemma" --> nodeC["P(n) ∈ ℂ^(N×N)"]
    nodeA -- "Cross with d*(n)" --> nodeD["p(n) ∈ ℂ^(N×1)"]
    nodeD -- "Multiply by P(n)" --> nodeE["w_hat(n) ∈ ℂ^(N×1)"]
    nodeC -- "Provides gain direction" --> nodeE
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the exponentially weighted least-squares cost function used in the RLS algorithm and explain the role of the forgetting factor $\lambda$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The RLS cost function at instant $n$ is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\big\vert e(i)\big\vert^{2}
$$

where $e(i) = d(i) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(i)$ is the estimation error and $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ is the **forgetting factor** (also called the exponential weighting factor).

**Role of $\lambda$:**
- When $\lambda = 1$, all past samples are equally weighted (infinite memory).
- When $\lambda < 1$, older samples are exponentially de-weighted, giving the filter a *finite effective memory* of approximately $\tfrac{1}{1-\lambda}$ samples.
- Smaller $\lambda$ → better tracking of non-stationary signals but higher steady-state misadjustment.
- Typical KTU value: $\lambda = 0.98$ or $0.99$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the significance of the Matrix Inversion Lemma in the formulation of the RLS algorithm?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Matrix Inversion Lemma (Woodbury Identity) is the cornerstone of RLS:

$$
(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{u}\mathbf{v}^H)^{-1} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} - \frac{\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}\mathbf{v}^H \mathbf{A}^{-1}}{1 + \mathbf{v}^H \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}}
$$

**Significance:**
1. It converts the expensive $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ matrix inversion $\mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)$ into a recursive $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ update, where $N$ is the filter order.
2. It enables the recursive update of $\mathbf{P}(n) = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)$ using only the previous inverse $\mathbf{P}(n-1)$ and the new sample $\mathbf{x}(n)$.
3. It allows the gain vector $\mathbf{k}(n)$ to be computed without forming $\mathbf{R}(n)$ explicitly.
4. Without this lemma, the RLS algorithm would be computationally infeasible for real-time applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the normal equations $\mathbf{R}(n)\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{p}(n)$ for the RLS algorithm starting from the exponentially weighted least-squares cost function. Define all terms used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Matrix Inversion Lemma, derive the recursive update for $\mathbf{P}(n) = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)$ and obtain the expression for the gain vector $\mathbf{k}(n)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Normal Equations Derivation:**

Starting from the cost function (refer Section 3.1 derivation above for the full expansion):

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\big\vert e(i)\big\vert^{2}, \quad e(i) = d(i) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{x}(i)
$$

Define the deterministic autocorrelation matrix and cross-correlation vector:

$$
\mathbf{R}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\mathbf{x}(i)\,\mathbf{x}^H(i), \qquad \mathbf{p}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\mathbf{x}(i)\,d^*(i)
$$

Expanding the cost function:

$$
\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\,\big\vert d(i)\big\vert^{2} - 2\,\text{Re}\{\hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n)\} + \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n)\,\mathbf{R}(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n)
$$

Setting the gradient $\nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{w}}^*}\mathcal{J}(n) = -\mathbf{p}(n) + \mathbf{R}(n)\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{R}(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{p}(n)\,}
$$

with direct solution $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n)$.

*(Allocate 1 mark each: cost function, definitions, expansion, gradient, final normal eqs.)*

**Part (b) — Recursive $\mathbf{P}(n)$ Update:**

Using $\mathbf{R}(n) = \lambda\,\mathbf{R}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)$, identify in the Woodbury identity:
- $\mathbf{A} = \lambda\,\mathbf{R}(n-1)$, so $\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{\lambda}\mathbf{P}(n-1)$
- $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{x}(n)$, $\mathbf{v}^H = \mathbf{x}^H(n)$

Applying the lemma:

$$
\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \frac{\tfrac{1}{\lambda^2}\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)}{1 + \tfrac{1}{\lambda}\mathbf{x}^H(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{x}(n)}
$$

Defining the gain vector:

$$
\mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}
$$

Final compact recursive form:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\big[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big]\,}
$$

*(Allocate marks: 2 for applying lemma, 2 for algebraic simplification, 2 for gain vector definition, 1 for final form.)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Starting from the matrix form of the normal equations and using the recursions $\mathbf{R}(n) = \lambda\mathbf{R}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)$ and $\mathbf{p}(n) = \lambda\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)d^*(n)$, derive the final RLS weight update equation:

$$\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\xi^*(n)$$

Clearly define $\mathbf{k}(n)$ and $\xi(n)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An RLS adaptive filter with $N = 2$ taps is operated with $\lambda = 0.95$. At time $n-1$, the state is $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{P}(n-1) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. The new input is $\mathbf{x}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and desired response $d(n) = 3$. Compute $\xi(n)$, $\mathbf{k}(n)$, $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n)$, and $\mathbf{P}(n)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Using $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n)$ and substituting $\mathbf{p}(n) = \lambda\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)d^*(n)$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n)\big[\lambda\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)d^*(n)\big]
$$

Substituting $\mathbf{P}(n) = \tfrac{1}{\lambda}\big[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big]$ and expanding (using $\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{p}(n-1) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1)$):

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\big[d^*(n) - \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1)\big]
$$

Define the **a priori error**:

$$
\xi(n) = d(n) - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

So $\xi^*(n) = d^*(n) - \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1)$, yielding:

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\xi^*(n)\,}
$$

where $\mathbf{k}(n) = \dfrac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\xi(n)$:**

$$
y(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}^H(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = [1\;\; 0]\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 1
$$

$$
\xi(n) = d(n) - y(n) = 3 - 1 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{k}(n)$:**

First, $\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$

Next, $\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = [1\;\; 1]\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = 4$

Denominator: $\lambda + 4 = 0.95 + 4 = 4.95$

$$
\mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{1}{4.95}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4040 \\ 0.4040 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Update $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n)$:**

Since $\xi^*(n) = 2$ (real):

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.4040 \\ 0.4040 \end{bmatrix} \times 2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1.8081 \\ 0.8081 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Update $\mathbf{P}(n)$:**

Compute $\mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1) = \mathbf{k}(n)\,[1\;\;1]\,\mathbf{P}(n-1)$:

$\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1) = [1\;\;1]\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = [2\;\; 2]$

$\mathbf{k}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^H(n)\,\mathbf{P}(n-1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4040 \\ 0.4040 \end{bmatrix}[2\;\; 2] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8081 & 0.8081 \\ 0.8081 & 0.8081 \end{bmatrix}$

$$
\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{0.95}\left[\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.8081 & 0.8081 \\ 0.8081 & 0.8081 \end{bmatrix}\right]
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{0.95}\begin{bmatrix} 1.1919 & -0.8081 \\ -0.8081 & 1.1919 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2547 & -0.8506 \\ -0.8506 & 1.2547 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take complex conjugate**: $\xi^*(n)$ vs $\xi(n)$. For real signals they coincide, but for complex they don't — examiners award zero if conjugates are flipped in derivations.
> 2. **Incorrect denominator**: Many students write $\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$ as $\lambda + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$ — must use Hermitian transpose $\mathbf{x}^H$.
> 3. **Mixing $e(n)$ and $\xi(n)$**: $\xi(n)$ is *a priori* (uses old weights); $e(n)$ is *a posteriori* (uses updated weights).
> 4. **Mis-application of Matrix Inversion Lemma**: The $1 + \mathbf{v}^H\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}$ in the denominator is a *scalar*, not a matrix.
> 5. **Initialisation forgetting**: Forgetting $\mathbf{P}(0) = \delta^{-1}\mathbf{I}$ causes numerical instability — always state initial conditions explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RLS Formulation**

- ✅ **Cost function**: $\mathcal{J}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\, \big\vert e(i)\big\vert^{2}$ — exponentially weighted LS.
- ✅ **Forgetting factor**: $\lambda \in (0, 1]$; smaller $\lambda$ → better tracking, higher misadjustment.
- ✅ **Normal equations**: $\mathbf{R}(n)\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \mathbf{p}(n)$ — optimality condition.
- ✅ **Recursions**: $\mathbf{R}(n) = \lambda\mathbf{R}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)$; $\mathbf{p}(n) = \lambda\mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{x}(n)d^*(n)$.
- ✅ **Matrix Inversion Lemma (Woodbury)** is the engine of RLS — converts $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ inversion into $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ recursion.
- ✅ **Gain vector**: $\mathbf{k}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{x}(n)/[\lambda + \mathbf{x}^H(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{x}(n)]$.
- ✅ **Weight update**: $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(n) = \hat{\mathbf{w}}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\xi^*(n)$.
- ✅ **Inverse update**: $\mathbf{P}(n) = \tfrac{1}{\lambda}\big[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\mathbf{x}^H(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\big]$.
- ✅ **Initialisation**: $\hat{\mathbf{w}}(0) = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{P}(0) = \delta^{-1}\mathbf{I}$, $\delta$ small and positive.
- ✅ **Convergence**: RLS converges in approximately $2N$ iterations, **independent** of eigenvalue spread of $\mathbf{R}$.
- ✅ **Complexity**: $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ per sample — trade-off for faster convergence vs LMS ($\mathcal{O}(N)$).
- ✅ **Error relation**: $e(n) = \xi(n)[1 - \mathbf{x}^H(n)\mathbf{k}(n)]$.
- ✅ **Variant**: When $\lambda = 1$, the algorithm reduces to the **standard LS solution** (no forgetting).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT752 - ADAPTIVE SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
